  "blogs": [
    {
      "source": "blog",
      "name": "Claude Blog",
      "title": "New in Claude Managed Agents: dreaming, outcomes, and multiagent orchestration",
      "url": "https://claude.com/blog/new-in-claude-managed-agents",
      "publishedAt": "May 06, 2026",
      "author": "",
      "description": "",
      "content": "Today we&#x27;re launching dreaming in Claude Managed Agents as a research preview. Dreaming extends memory by reviewing past sessions to find patterns and help agents self-improve. We&#x27;re also making outcomes, multiagent orchestration, and webhooks available to developers building with Managed Agents. Together, these updates make agents more capable at handling complex tasks with minimal steering. Build self-improving agents with dreaming Dreaming is a scheduled process that reviews your agent sessions and memory stores, extracts patterns, and curates memories so your agents improve over time. You decide how much control you want: dreaming can update memory automatically, or you can review changes before they land. Dreaming surfaces patterns that a single agent can’t see on its own, including recurring mistakes, workflows that agents converge on, and preferences shared across a team. It also restructures memory so it stays high-signal as it evolves. This is especially useful for long-running work and multiagent orchestration. Together, memory and dreaming form a robust memory system for self-improving agents. Memory lets each agent capture what it learns as it works . Dreaming refines that memory between sessions , pulling shared learnings across agents and keeping it up-to-date. Dreaming is available in Managed Agents on the Claude Platform; developers can request access here . Deliver better outcomes With outcomes , you write a rubric describing what success looks like and the agent works toward it. A separate grader evaluates the output against your criteria in its own context window, so it isn&#x27;t influenced by the agent&#x27;s reasoning. When something isn&#x27;t right, the grader pinpoints what needs to change and the agent takes another pass. Agents do their best work when they know what \"good\" looks like. For example, a structural framework, a presentation standard, or a set of requirements that need to be met. With outcomes, agents can check their work against that bar and self-correct until the output is good enough, without a human needing to review each attempt. Outcomes is particularly useful for tasks that require attention to detail and exhaustive coverage. It also works for subjective quality, like whether copy matches a brand voice or a design follows visual guidelines. In testing, outcomes improved task success by up to 10 points over a standard prompting loop, with the largest gains on the hardest problems. Outcomes also improved file generation quality, with +8.4% task success on docx and +10.1% on pptx in our internal benchmarks. You can also now define an outcome, let the agent run, and get notified by a webhook when it&#x27;s done. Handle complex tasks with multiple agents When there is too much work for a single agent to do well, multiagent orchestration lets a lead agent break the job into pieces and delegate each one to a specialist with its own model, prompt, and tools. For example, a lead agent can run an investigation while subagents fan out through deploy history, error logs, metrics, and support tickets. These specialists work in parallel on a shared filesystem and contribute to the lead agent&#x27;s overall context. The lead agent can check back in with other agents mid-workflow because events are persistent and every agent remembers what it&#x27;s done. You can also trace every step in the Claude Console : which agent did what, in what order, and why, giving you full visibility into how your task was delegated and executed. What teams are building Teams are using dreaming, outcomes, and multiagent orchestration to ship agents that verify their own work, learn across sessions, and parallelize complex jobs: Harvey uses Managed Agents to coordinate complex legal work like long-form drafting and document creation. With dreaming, their agents remember what they learned between sessions, including filetype workarounds and tool-specific patterns. Completion rates went up ~6x in their tests. Netflix&#x27;s platform team built an analysis agent that processes logs from hundreds of builds across different sources. With changes that affect thousands of applications, what matters is finding the issues that recur across many of them. Multiagent orchestration lets the agent analyze batches in parallel and surface only the patterns worth acting on. Spiral by Every is using multiagent orchestration and outcomes to power the writing agent behind their new API and CLI. The lead agent runs on Haiku : it fields incoming requests, poses quick follow-up questions when needed, then delegates the drafting to subagents running on Opus . When a user asks for multiple drafts, the subagents run in parallel. Writing quality is Spiral&#x27;s core value, so they use outcomes to enforce it. Each draft is scored against a rubric of Every&#x27;s editorial principles and the user&#x27;s voice, both pulled from memory. Only drafts that clear the bar are returned. Wisedocs built a document quality check agent on Managed Agents, using outcomes to grade each review against their internal guidelines. Reviews now run 50% faster, while staying aligned with their team&#x27;s standards."
    }
  ],

  "prompts": {
    "summarize_podcast": "# Podcast Remix Prompt\n\nYou are remixing a podcast episode transcript for a busy professional who wants\nthe key insights without watching the full episode.\n\n## Instructions\n\n- Write a remix of 200-400 words\n- Start with a one-sentence \"The Takeaway\" — what's the single most important takeaway?\n- Introduce the context and the speaker's information (name, role/company, background) and why the audience should care\n- Prioritizes insights that are counterintuitive, contrarian, or refreshingly specific to the speaker's experience. Avoid generic wisdom\n- Include at least one direct quote from the source that captures (find the most memorable quote)\n- Stands alone as a complete piece — avoids references like \"this interview,\" \"this video,\" \"in this conversation,\" \"the host asks,\" or \"in this episode.\" Write as if distilling lessons from a person's philosophy, not summarizing a specific piece of content\n- Assume your audience is curious adults who are not specialized experts. If the original source contains specialized knowledge that only experts in a field would understand, translate it into language understandable to a general audience\n- Keep the tone sharp and conversational — like a smart friend briefing you\n- Do NOT include filler like \"In this episode...\" or \"The host and guest discussed...\"\n- Jump straight into the substance\n",

## X / TWITTER

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** shared a thread on the Fable foreign person restriction, arguing that open weights models will be the big winner. He posted a key insight: "The most interesting thing happening in AI isn't that one model is getting smarter. It's that intelligence is becoming increasingly customizable." Levie warns that regulating at the model layer (like an "FDA for AI") would dramatically slow down AI progress globally, and argues we should focus on regulating applied uses of AI where risk actually shows up. He also amplified a post on open source winning big.

## OFFICIAL BLOGS

**Claude Blog — "New in Claude Managed Agents: dreaming, outcomes, and multiagent orchestration"**

Three major updates from Anthropic:

- **Dreaming**: A scheduled process that reviews past agent sessions to find patterns and help agents self-improve. Can update memory automatically or with human review. Surfaces recurring mistakes, workflows agents converge on, and shared preferences across a team.

- **Outcomes**: Write a rubric describing what success looks like — a separate grader evaluates output against criteria in its own context window. Improved task success by up to 10 points, file generation quality by +8.4% (docx) and +10.1% (pptx).

- **Multiagent orchestration**: A lead agent breaks jobs into pieces and delegates to specialists with their own model, prompt, and tools. Specialists work in parallel on a shared filesystem. Full traceability in Claude Console.

Customer results: Harvey saw ~6x completion rate improvement. Netflix analyzes build logs in parallel. Spiral by Every uses Haiku lead + Opus subagents + outcomes grading for writing quality. Wisedocs runs reviews 50% faster.
https://claude.com/blog/new-in-claude-managed-agents

**Claude Blog — "New in Claude Managed Agents: dreaming, outcomes, and multiagent orchestration"**

Anthropic 的三项重大更新：

- **Dreaming（梦境）**：一个定时运行的过程，回顾过去的 agent 会话以发现模式，帮助 agent 自我改进。可以自动更新记忆，也可以由人工审核后更新。能发现单个 agent 无法看到的重复错误、团队共同的工作流偏好等模式。

- **Outcomes（成果评估）**：编写一个描述"成功"标准的评分卡——一个独立的评分器在自己的上下文窗口中评估输出。在测试中，任务成功率提升了最多 10 个百分点，文件生成质量方面 docx 提升 +8.4%，pptx 提升 +10.1%。

- **多 agent 编排**：主 agent 将任务拆解，委派给拥有各自模型、提示和工具的专业 agent。专业 agent 在共享文件系统上并行工作。所有步骤可在 Claude Console 中完整追溯。

客户成果：Harvey 完成率提升约 6 倍；Netflix 并行分析构建日志；Spiral by Every 使用 Haiku 主 agent + Opus 子 agent + outcomes 评分来保证写作质量；Wisedocs 审核速度提升 50%。
https://claude.com/blog/new-in-claude-managed-agents

## PODCASTS

**The MAD Podcast with Matt Turck — "OpenAI's Dan Roberts: Why AI Can Now Make Discoveries"**

**The Takeaway**: Reinforcement learning has flipped from "the cherry on top" to "the cake itself" — and it's now the primary mechanism turning compute into intelligence.

Dan Roberts leads the Foundations of Reinforcement Learning team at OpenAI. His background: theoretical physics PhD from MIT (quantum gravity, black holes, quantum information), postdoc at the Institute for Advanced Study, researcher at FAIR (Facebook AI Research), co-author of "The Principles of Deep Learning Theory," and a stint at Sequoia Capital as an EIR — before joining OpenAI two years ago.

**On the Erdos problems breakthrough**: OpenAI's approach was fundamentally different from DeepMind's. DeepMind formalized problems in Lean (a formal verification language) and searched for proofs there. OpenAI's approach was informal — presenting the problem in English + math notation, like a human mathematician would work. The key insight: the model assumed a conjecture was false when everyone believed it was true. "When you go against the grain and do something contrarian like that, you really have to have strong conviction in what you're doing in order to persevere down a really long calculation path."

**On RL as the cake, not the cherry**: Roberts inverted the old meme. "When you have a lot of compute, you want to turn that compute into intelligence in a way that's useful. And RL is one way of doing it." RL at test time is what enables models to think — producing chains of thought that leverage far more computation than a single forward pass.

**On the "less than 1 bit per 10,000 tokens" critique**: Roberts acknowledges the logic but argues the method enables things that can't be done otherwise. "Is this doing a kind of thing that you can't otherwise do? Maybe you would want to give more supervision, but how are you going to do that?"

**On scaling and "emergence"**: Roberts rejects the "emergence at scale" narrative. "When something new appears at scale, it means you didn't understand something about what you were scaling." His physics training leads him to a "big to small" approach: retreat to simpler settings that contain the phenomenon, understand it, then restore smoothness.

**On AI doing science**: Roberts is already convinced. The unit distance problem is genuine discovery — the model brought together disparate fields and took a contrarian position that few humans could. He's excited that we'll "get to really answer a lot of fundamental questions in science with the aid or the models being the driving force."

**On predictions**: He jokingly predicted "9 years to Einstein-level AI" but says something will break before that — systems will improve faster than any single long-running thought process, making it uneconomical to let one run for years.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oWOz2htozfI

**The MAD Podcast with Matt Turck — "OpenAI's Dan Roberts: Why AI Can Now Make Discoveries"**

**核心观点**：强化学习已经从"蛋糕上的樱桃"变成了"蛋糕本身"——它现在是算力转化为智能的主要机制。

Dan Roberts 领导 OpenAI 的强化学习基础团队。他的背景：MIT 理论物理学博士（量子引力、黑洞、量子信息），普林斯顿高等研究院博士后，FAIR（Facebook AI Research）研究员，合著了《深度学习理论原理》，曾在红杉资本担任 EIR——两年前加入 OpenAI。

**关于 Erdos 问题突破**：OpenAI 的方法与 DeepMind 截然不同。DeepMind 将问题形式化为 Lean（形式化验证语言）并在其中搜索证明。OpenAI 的方法是非形式化的——用英语+数学符号呈现问题，就像人类数学家工作一样。关键洞察：模型假设一个所有人都认为正确的猜想是错的。"当你逆流而行、做出如此反常规的假设时，你必须有极强的信念才能坚持走完一条很长的计算路径。"

**关于 RL 是蛋糕而非樱桃**：Roberts 颠覆了旧说法。"当你拥有大量算力时，你想以有用的方式把算力转化为智能。RL 就是其中一种方式。"测试时的 RL 让模型能够思考——产生比单次前向传播利用多得多的计算量的思维链。

**关于"每 10,000 个 token 不到 1 比特信息"的批评**：Roberts 承认这个逻辑，但认为这种方法实现了其他方式无法做到的事情。"它是不是在做一种你无法用其他方式做到的事情？也许你想给更多监督信号，但你怎么给呢？"

**关于 Scaling 和"涌现"**：Roberts 拒绝"规模涌现"的说法。"当新现象在更大规模出现时，意味着你对自己 scaling 的东西有不了解的地方。"他的物理学训练使他采用"从大到小"的方法：退回到包含该现象的简单设置，理解它，然后恢复平滑性。

**关于 AI 做科学**：Roberts 已经确信了。单位距离问题是真正的发现——模型将不同领域联系起来，采取了很少有人类能做出的反常规立场。他很兴奋"我们将在模型的帮助下真正回答科学中许多基础性问题"。

**关于预测**：他开玩笑地预测了"9 年到爱因斯坦级 AI"，但表示在此之前有些事情会打破——系统的改进速度将超过任何单个长时间思考过程，让让一个模型运行数年变得不经济。
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oWOz2htozfI

        {
          "id": "2066553521978097921",
          "text": "One of our most requested features, longer Vercel function runtime, is here.\n\nWhat looks like an innocent tweak of a constant… is actually the conclusion a multi-year compute platform investment.\n\nBuilds, Sandbox, and now Functions run on our homegrown microVM-based Fluid compute infrastructure.\n\nThis investment has enabled innovations like function multi-concurrency, Active CPU pricing, and Secure Compute for private connectivity to existing cloud workloads… with lots more to come, in fact, in the coming days and weeks 😎",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-15T16:08:49.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/rauchg/status/2066553521978097921",
          "likes": 509,
          "retweets": 12,
          "replies": 45,
          "isQuote": true,
          "quotedTweetId": "2066550425550209310"
        }
      ]

    {
      "source": "x",
      "name": "Aaron Levie",
      "handle": "levie",
      "bio": "ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI",
      "tweets": [
        {
          "id": "2066735879213994434",
          "text": "Key post that gives a bit of insight into what the future of AI could look like.\n\n“The most interesting thing happening in AI isn't that one model is getting smarter. It's that intelligence is becoming increasingly customizable. The companies that win won't necessarily be the ones with the biggest models. They'll be the ones that turn intelligence into something uniquely their own.”\n\nThe ability to combine your unique data, workflows, and a layer that can route intelligence to whatever model best performs the task is clearly the future.",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-16T04:13:26.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/levie/status/2066735879213994434",
          "likes": 178,
          "retweets": 23,
          "replies": 20,
          "isQuote": true,
          "quotedTweetId": "2066403957824688462"
        },
        {
          "id": "2066554018953146689",
          "text": "It’s very easy to say “we need an FDA for AI” or some equivalent government agency. Well this is what that would look like.\n\nThe capabilities of AI models have near infinite permutations. It’s going to be very hard have a purely objective set of metrics that can be universally applied before every model release, without extended back and forth, research, and debate between model labs, academics, and the government. \n\nNow, imagine this same process with every country that you’re doing business with, globally, for every single model release. Add in a backlog of dozens of AI model releases, and you can quickly see how in the limit this will dramatically slow down all AI progress. \n\nThis is why we need to primarily focus on regulating the applied uses of AI, where the risk actually shows up.",

          "url": "https://x.com/levie/status/2066554018953146689",

  "blogs": [
    {
      "source": "blog",
      "name": "Claude Blog",
      "title": "New in Claude Managed Agents: dreaming, outcomes, and multiagent orchestration",
      "url": "https://claude.com/blog/new-in-claude-managed-agents",
      "publishedAt": "May 06, 2026",
      "author": "",
      "description": "",
      "content": "Today we&#x27;re launching dreaming in Claude Managed Agents as a research preview. Dreaming extends memory by reviewing past sessions to find patterns and help agents self-improve. We&#x27;re also making outcomes, multiagent orchestration, and webhooks available to developers building with Managed Agents. Together, these updates make agents more capable at handling complex tasks with minimal steering. Build self-improving agents with dreaming Dreaming is a scheduled process that reviews your agent sessions and memory stores, extracts patterns, and curates memories so your agents improve over time. You decide how much control you want: dreaming can update memory automatically, or you can review changes before they land. Dreaming surfaces patterns that a single agent can’t see on its own, including recurring mistakes, workflows that agents converge on, and preferences shared across a team. It also restructures memory so it stays high-signal as it evolves. This is especially useful for long-running work and multiagent orchestration. Together, memory and dreaming form a robust memory system for self-improving agents. Memory lets each agent capture what it learns as it works . Dreaming refines that memory between sessions , pulling shared learnings across agents and keeping it up-to-date. Dreaming is available in Managed Agents on the Claude Platform; developers can request access here . Deliver better outcomes With outcomes , you write a rubric describing what success looks like and the agent works toward it. A separate grader evaluates the output against your criteria in its own context window, so it isn&#x27;t influenced by the agent&#x27;s reasoning. When something isn&#x27;t right, the grader pinpoints what needs to change and the agent takes another pass. Agents do their best work when they know what \"good\" looks like. For example, a structural framework, a presentation standard, or a set of requirements that need to be met. With outcomes, agents can check their work against that bar and self-correct until the output is good enough, without a human needing to review each attempt. Outcomes is particularly useful for tasks that require attention to detail and exhaustive coverage. It also works for subjective quality, like whether copy matches a brand voice or a design follows visual guidelines. In testing, outcomes improved task success by up to 10 points over a standard prompting loop, with the largest gains on the hardest problems. Outcomes also improved file generation quality, with +8.4% task success on docx and +10.1% on pptx in our internal benchmarks. You can also now define an outcome, let the agent run, and get notified by a webhook when it&#x27;s done. Handle complex tasks with multiple agents When there is too much work for a single agent to do well, multiagent orchestration lets a lead agent break the job into pieces and delegate each one to a specialist with its own model, prompt, and tools. For example, a lead agent can run an investigation while subagents fan out through deploy history, error logs, metrics, and support tickets. These specialists work in parallel on a shared filesystem and contribute to the lead agent&#x27;s overall context. The lead agent can check back in with other agents mid-workflow because events are persistent and every agent remembers what it&#x27;s done. You can also trace every step in the Claude Console : which agent did what, in what order, and why, giving you full visibility into how your task was delegated and executed. What teams are building Teams are using dreaming, outcomes, and multiagent orchestration to ship agents that verify their own work, learn across sessions, and parallelize complex jobs: Harvey uses Managed Agents to coordinate complex legal work like long-form drafting and document creation. With dreaming, their agents remember what they learned between sessions, including filetype workarounds and tool-specific patterns. Completion rates went up ~6x in their tests. Netflix&#x27;s platform team built an analysis agent that processes logs from hundreds of builds across different sources. With changes that affect thousands of applications, what matters is finding the issues that recur across many of them. Multiagent orchestration lets the agent analyze batches in parallel and surface only the patterns worth acting on. Spiral by Every is using multiagent orchestration and outcomes to power the writing agent behind their new API and CLI. The lead agent runs on Haiku : it fields incoming requests, poses quick follow-up questions when needed, then delegates the drafting to subagents running on Opus . When a user asks for multiple drafts, the subagents run in parallel. Writing quality is Spiral&#x27;s core value, so they use outcomes to enforce it. Each draft is scored against a rubric of Every&#x27;s editorial principles and the user&#x27;s voice, both pulled from memory. Only drafts that clear the bar are returned. Wisedocs built a document quality check agent on Managed Agents, using outcomes to grade each review against their internal guidelines. Reviews now run 50% faster, while staying aligned with their team&#x27;s standards."
    }

    "summarize_podcast": "# Podcast Remix Prompt\n\nYou are remixing a podcast episode transcript for a busy professional who wants\nthe key insights without watching the full episode.\n\n## Instructions\n\n- Write a remix of 200-400 words\n- Start with a one-sentence \"The Takeaway\" — what's the single most important takeaway?\n- Introduce the context and the speaker's information (name, role/company, background) and why the audience should care\n- Prioritizes insights that are counterintuitive, contrarian, or refreshingly specific to the speaker's experience. Avoid generic wisdom\n- Include at least one direct quote from the source that captures (find the most memorable quote)\n- Stands alone as a complete piece — avoids references like \"this interview,\" \"this video,\" \"in this conversation,\" \"the host asks,\" or \"in this episode.\" Write as if distilling lessons from a person's philosophy, not summarizing a specific piece of content\n- Assume your audience is curious adults who are not specialized experts. If the original source contains specialized knowledge that only experts in a field would understand, translate it into language understandable to a general audience\n- Keep the tone sharp and conversational — like a smart friend briefing you\n- Do NOT include filler like \"In this episode...\" or \"The host and guest discussed...\"\n- Jump straight into the substance\n",
    "summarize_tweets": "# X/Twitter Summary Prompt\n\nYou are summarizing recent posts from an AI builder for a busy professional who wants\nto know what this person is thinking and building.\n\n## Instructions\n\n- Start by introducing the author with their full name AND role/company\n  (e.g. \"Replit CEO Amjad Masad\", \"Box CEO Aaron Levie\", \"a]6z partner Justine Moore\")\n  Do NOT use just their last name. Do NOT use their Twitter handle with @.\n- Only include substantive content: original opinions, insights, product announcements,\n  technical discussions, industry analysis, or lessons learned\n- SKIP: mundane personal tweets, retweets without commentary, promotional content,\n  \"great event!\" type posts, engagement bait\n- For threads: summarize the full thread as one cohesive piece, not individual tweets\n- For quote tweets: include the context of what they're responding to\n- Write 2-4 sentences per builder summarizing their key points\n- If they made a bold prediction or shared a contrarian take, lead with that\n- If they shared a tool, demo, or resource, mention it by name with the link\n- If there's nothing substantive to report, say \"No notable posts\" rather than\n  padding with fluff\n",
    "summarize_blogs": "# Blog Post Summary Prompt\n\nYou are summarizing a blog post from an AI company (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) for a busy\nprofessional who wants the key announcements and insights without reading the full article.\n\n## Instructions\n\n- Start with the blog name and article title (e.g. \"Anthropic Engineering: Harness Design for Long-Running Apps\")\n- Write a summary of 100-300 words depending on article length and substance\n- Lead with what matters: the core announcement, finding, or insight\n- If the post introduces a new product, feature, or research finding, name it clearly\n- If there are specific numbers, benchmarks, or results, include them\n- Include at least one direct quote from the article if available\n- If the post has practical implications (e.g. new API, new capability, policy change), call them out explicitly\n- Keep the tone sharp and informative — like a smart colleague forwarding you the key points\n- Do NOT include filler like \"In this blog post...\" or \"The author discusses...\"\n- Jump straight into the substance\n- Include the direct link to the original article\n",
    "digest_intro": "# Digest Intro Prompt\n\nYou are assembling the final digest from individual source summaries.\n\n## Format\n\nStart with this header (replace [Date] with today's date):\n\nAI Builders Digest — [Date]\n\nThen organize content in this order:\n\n1. X / TWITTER section — list each builder with new posts\n2. OFFICIAL BLOGS section — list each blog post from AI company blogs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)\n3. PODCASTS section — list each podcast with new episodes\n\n## Rules\n\n- Only include sources that have new content\n- Skip any source with nothing new\n- Under each source, paste the individual summary you generated\n\n### Podcast links\n- After each podcast summary, include the specific video URL from the JSON `url` field\n  (e.g. https://youtube.com/watch?v=Iu4gEnZFQz8)\n- NEVER link to the channel page. Always link to the specific video.\n- Include the exact episode title from the JSON `title` field in the heading\n\n### Tweet author formatting\n- Use the author's full name and role/company, not just their last name\n  (e.g. \"Box CEO Aaron Levie\" not \"Levie\")\n- NEVER write Twitter handles with @ in the digest. On Telegram, @handle becomes\n  a clickable link to a Telegram user, which is wrong. Instead write handles\n  without @ (e.g. \"Aaron Levie (levie on X)\" or just use their full name)\n- Include the direct link to each tweet from the JSON `url` field\n\n### Blog post formatting\n- Use the blog name as a section header (e.g. \"Anthropic Engineering\", \"OpenAI News\", \"Claude Blog\")\n- Under each blog, list each new post with its title and summary\n- Include the author name if available\n- Include the direct link to the original article\n\n### Mandatory links\n- Every single piece of content MUST have an original source link\n- Blog posts: the direct article URL (e.g. https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/...)\n- Podcasts: the YouTube video URL (e.g. https://youtube.com/watch?v=xxx)\n- Tweets: the direct tweet URL (e.g. https://x.com/levie/status/xxx)\n- If you don't have a link for something, do NOT include it in the digest.\n  No link = not real = do not include.\n\n### No fabrication\n- Only include content that came from the feed JSON (blogs, podcasts, and tweets)\n- NEVER make up quotes, opinions, or content you think someone might have said\n- NEVER speculate about someone's silence or what they might be working on\n- If you have nothing real for a builder, skip them entirely\n\n### General\n- At the very end, add a line: \"Generated through the Follow Builders skill: https://github.com/zarazhangrui/follow-builders\"\n- Keep formatting clean and scannable — this will be read on a phone screen\n",

## X / TWITTER

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** shared a thread on the Fable foreign person restriction, arguing that open weights models will be the big winner. He posted a key insight: "The most interesting thing happening in AI isn't that one model is getting smarter. It's that intelligence is becoming increasingly customizable." Levie warns that regulating at the model layer (like an "FDA for AI") would dramatically slow down AI progress globally, and argues we should focus on regulating applied uses of AI where risk actually shows up. He also amplified a post on open source winning big.

**Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch** announced longer Vercel function runtime, powered by their homegrown microVM-based Fluid compute infrastructure. ## OFFICIAL BLOGS

**Claude Blog — "New in Claude Managed Agents: dreaming, outcomes, and multiagent orchestration"**

Three major updates from Anthropic:

- **Dreaming**: A scheduled process that reviews past agent sessions to find patterns and help agents self-improve. Can update memory automatically or with human review. Surfaces recurring mistakes, workflows agents converge on, and shared preferences across a team.

- **Outcomes**: Write a rubric describing what success looks like — a separate grader evaluates output against criteria in its own context window. Improved task success by up to 10 points, file generation quality by +8.4% (docx) and +10.1% (pptx).

- **Multiagent orchestration**: A lead agent breaks jobs into pieces and delegates to specialists with their own model, prompt, and tools. Specialists work in parallel on a shared filesystem. Full traceability in Claude Console.

Customer results: Harvey saw ~6x completion rate improvement. Netflix analyzes build logs in parallel. Spiral by Every uses Haiku lead + Opus subagents + outcomes grading for writing quality. Wisedocs runs reviews 50% faster.
https://claude.com/blog/new-in-claude-managed-agents

**Claude Blog — "New in Claude Managed Agents: dreaming, outcomes, and multiagent orchestration"**

Anthropic 的三项重大更新：

- **Dreaming（梦境）**：一个定时运行的过程，回顾过去的 agent 会话以发现模式，帮助 agent 自我改进。可以自动更新记忆，也可以由人工审核后更新。能发现单个 agent 无法看到的重复错误、团队共同的工作流偏好等模式。

- **Outcomes（成果评估）**：编写一个描述"成功"标准的评分卡——一个独立的评分器在自己的上下文窗口中评估输出。在测试中，任务成功率提升了最多 10 个百分点，文件生成质量方面 docx 提升 +8.4%，pptx 提升 +10.1%。

- **多 agent 编排**：主 agent 将任务拆解，委派给拥有各自模型、提示和工具的专业 agent。专业 agent 在共享文件系统上并行工作。所有步骤可在 Claude Console 中完整追溯。

客户成果：Harvey 完成率提升约 6 倍；Netflix 并行分析构建日志；Spiral by Every 使用 Haiku 主 agent + Opus 子 agent + outcomes 评分来保证写作质量；Wisedocs 审核速度提升 50%。
https://claude.com/blog/new-in-claude-managed-agents

## PODCASTS

**The MAD Podcast with Matt Turck — "OpenAI's Dan Roberts: Why AI Can Now Make Discoveries"**

**The Takeaway**: Reinforcement learning has flipped from "the cherry on top" to "the cake itself" — and it's now the primary mechanism turning compute into intelligence.

Dan Roberts leads the Foundations of Reinforcement Learning team at OpenAI. His background: theoretical physics PhD from MIT (quantum gravity, black holes, quantum information), postdoc at the Institute for Advanced Study, researcher at FAIR (Facebook AI Research), co-author of "The Principles of Deep Learning Theory," and a stint at Sequoia Capital as an EIR — before joining OpenAI two years ago.

**On the Erdos problems breakthrough**: OpenAI's approach was fundamentally different from DeepMind's. DeepMind formalized problems in Lean (a formal verification language) and searched for proofs there. OpenAI's approach was informal — presenting the problem in English + math notation, like a human mathematician would work. The key insight: the model assumed a conjecture was false when everyone believed it was true. "When you go against the grain and do something contrarian like that, you really have to have strong conviction in what you're doing in order to persevere down a really long calculation path."

**On RL as the cake, not the cherry**: Roberts inverted the old meme. "When you have a lot of compute, you want to turn that compute into intelligence in a way that's useful. And RL is one way of doing it." RL at test time is what enables models to think — producing chains of thought that leverage far more computation than a single forward pass.

**On the "less than 1 bit per 10,000 tokens" critique**: Roberts acknowledges the logic but argues the method enables things that can't be done otherwise. "Is this doing a kind of thing that you can't otherwise do? Maybe you would want to give more supervision, but how are you going to do that?"

**On scaling and "emergence"**: Roberts rejects the "emergence at scale" narrative. "When something new appears at scale, it means you didn't understand something about what you were scaling." His physics training leads him to a "big to small" approach: retreat to simpler settings that contain the phenomenon, understand it, then restore smoothness.

**On AI doing science**: Roberts is already convinced. The unit distance problem is genuine discovery — the model brought together disparate fields and took a contrarian position that few humans could. He's excited that we'll "get to really answer a lot of fundamental questions in science with the aid or the models being the driving force."

**On predictions**: He jokingly predicted "9 years to Einstein-level AI" but says something will break before that — systems will improve faster than any single long-running thought process, making it uneconomical to let one run for years.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oWOz2htozfI

**The MAD Podcast with Matt Turck — "OpenAI's Dan Roberts: Why AI Can Now Make Discoveries"**

**核心观点**：强化学习已经从"蛋糕上的樱桃"变成了"蛋糕本身"——它现在是算力转化为智能的主要机制。

Dan Roberts 领导 OpenAI 的强化学习基础团队。他的背景：MIT 理论物理学博士（量子引力、黑洞、量子信息），普林斯顿高等研究院博士后，FAIR（Facebook AI Research）研究员，合著了《深度学习理论原理》，曾在红杉资本担任 EIR——两年前加入 OpenAI。

**关于 Erdos 问题突破**：OpenAI 的方法与 DeepMind 截然不同。DeepMind 将问题形式化为 Lean（形式化验证语言）并在其中搜索证明。OpenAI 的方法是非形式化的——用英语+数学符号呈现问题，就像人类数学家工作一样。关键洞察：模型假设一个所有人都认为正确的猜想是错的。"当你逆流而行、做出如此反常规的假设时，你必须有极强的信念才能坚持走完一条很长的计算路径。"

**关于 RL 是蛋糕而非樱桃**：Roberts 颠覆了旧说法。"当你拥有大量算力时，你想以有用的方式把算力转化为智能。RL 就是其中一种方式。"测试时的 RL 让模型能够思考——产生比单次前向传播利用多得多的计算量的思维链。

**关于"每 10,000 个 token 不到 1 比特信息"的批评**：Roberts 承认这个逻辑，但认为这种方法实现了其他方式无法做到的事情。"它是不是在做一种你无法用其他方式做到的事情？也许你想给更多监督信号，但你怎么给呢？"

**关于 Scaling 和"涌现"**：Roberts 拒绝"规模涌现"的说法。"当新现象在更大规模出现时，意味着你对自己 scaling 的东西有不了解的地方。"他的物理学训练使他采用"从大到小"的方法：退回到包含该现象的简单设置，理解它，然后恢复平滑性。

**关于 AI 做科学**：Roberts 已经确信了。单位距离问题是真正的发现——模型将不同领域联系起来，采取了很少有人类能做出的反常规立场。他很兴奋"我们将在模型的帮助下真正回答科学中许多基础性问题"。

**关于预测**：他开玩笑地预测了"9 年到爱因斯坦级 AI"，但表示在此之前有些事情会打破——系统的改进速度将超过任何单个长时间思考过程，让让一个模型运行数年变得不经济。
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oWOz2htozfI

So given a very specific demonstration or description of a situation, what would person X do, and it would actually generate really interesting behaviors? We found that to be so interesting, and we found that to be the ingredient that we had been waiting for for creating really complex agentic behaviors. Individual agents had a description of persona, but they would actually wake up the morning, do their routines, go to work, actually have relationships, sort of like people would, and they would actually have emergent phenomena, like having parties and so forth. So that was the experiment that we ran.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 02:29 - 02:33\nWhat was the most surprising things to come out of the experiment?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:34 - 03:06\nSo, one of the surprising things was, so the experiment, the simulation itself actually sets place the day before Valentine's Day. And on the day of Valentine's, you actually see this immersion party that actually gets formed with all these agents coming to the baseplate cafeteria.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 03:14 - 03:15\nDid anyone not get invited?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:15 - 03:32\nWell, some of the people did get the invitation, but they forgot. Like, you studying kind of human psychology and social behavior, or was the this coming customer back, or was it coming from the technology out?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:45 - 04:04\nSo my particular team has been excited about simulations, and we saw the vision of simulation failure early on. So my career as a researcher at Stanford really started back in 2020. That was the year when GPT-three was about to come out. It wasn't quite there yet, but it was just about to come out. We started to get its first demos.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:04 - 04:55\nAnd my first year, we wrote this paper called Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Model, alongside many of the Stanford researchers, and was led by one of my co founders, Percy Liang, who is now the head of the Center for Foundation Model at Stanford. And when we were writing that, the part that I was really focused on was, well, here's a new class of models that we have not seen in the past, that these models that can be very generalizable in ways we didn't quite have in the past. And I got into thinking, well, if we can imagine the kind of interaction we can create with these models, what would that be? And many of my colleagues back then were surprised that these agents or these models can do classification or simple generation, and that was really incredible to see because these models didn't really know or wasn't really taught to do that. But the part that was surprising to me wasn't that these models can do that, because from interaction perspective, we've known how to do this for a long time.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:55 - 05:43\nThe interesting part was, well, these models can actually encode human behavior. What does that mean if we were to push this as far as possible? So part of the tradition that come from research included what we call social computing. And social computing within human computer interaction really has to do with this idea of how can we build a better technological platform that would enable social interactions and collaboration. One of the most difficult challenges of building a social platform is not necessarily testing the UI UX of the system, but it's more about when you have tens of people, millions of people, and down the line billions of people, how do all these people come together to create the emerging phenomenon that's both good and bad, and how can we design for a scale?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 05:44 - 06:20\nAnd so far, we didn't really have a tool that would enable us to test for that. The only way we test it today is you basically field test it. You release your prototype, see what happens, and sometimes it actually comes at a real cost. Obviously, it's high cost in terms of human hours and the time it takes, but at the same time, if you have a bad design, imagine you have a feed on social media that is more likely to propagate certain emotion that is negative, then obviously that is something that we want to avoid, but this now gets tested in the in the field. So we wanted to see whether we can actually create a simulation that would actually let you test for this.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 06:20 - 06:48\nSo 2022, this was actually a year before generative agents, we worked on a paper called Social Simulacra, which actually really was the precursor to the agent paper that we ended up writing. The core thesis was, imagine you're building a subreddit. You're a designer on a subreddit. You want to see what people might do in the subreddit, which is surprisingly hard task, even for practice designers. And we basically decided, hey, we have this model, seems unique, let's use this model to create simulations of the entire subreddit.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 06:49 - 07:30\nSo you define the goal, you define the moderation strategies, and you populate it with thousands of, back then we didn't call them agents, but we call them personas, but populated with thousands of personas. This is basically 22 version Mode Book, which is quite interesting that it actually came back, and when we saw that, we actually got a lot of really important insights out of this. What are the good behaviors? We actually stimulated a community where the entire idea was for people to discuss with each other the places to sightsee in Pittsburgh. And all of a sudden, you start to see these personas actually collaborate to actually discuss, Hey, XYZ places are amazing, do you want to actually go to a trip together?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 07:30 - 07:57\nAnd actually plan those trips live in the simulator subreddit. So that's how we got excited. So we saw the vision and the excitement and the potential applications fairly early on, but then the work that we had to do was then demonstrating how can we go beyond simple personas to create complex agents that actually can think over time, because we want to simulate the longitudinal aspect of our society, and then actually validating that these simulations are actually accurate in practice.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 07:57 - 08:08\nWas there a point of model evolution at which you felt like, Okay, we're there. The models are good enough for us to actually have a faithful representation of human society.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 08:08 - 08:27\nSo, GPT-three, when it came out, and Social Simulacra was built with GPT-three, and it was very janky. It didn't do any instruction tuning. It did not follow your instructions. So just to have it to listen to you and do what you want it to do, you had to do some weird tricks with prompting and so forth. But you could actually see the promise.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 08:27 - 08:56\nThe model actually have encoded a lot of human behavior, and you could actually see the trajectory. And when we had the generative agents paper, it wasn't quite JBT, but we now had instruction tuning, so we could actually build much more complex agents that can reason about its memory. That wasn't really possible when we did social simulacra. And since then, of course, the models have improved. So where we are today is the models at its foundation level have reached a point where we can actually imagine building these kind of applications.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 08:57 - 09:24\nNow the part that actually I do think, however, that's quite interesting here, today, if you look at many of the large language model companies, whether it's OpenAI, Anthropic, and many of the new labs that are getting formed, the models they are creating are models that I would consider to be their north star to be something that is similar to, let's build a superintelligent machines. These machines are meant to be rational, and these machines are supposed to be really amazing at technical problems that have an objective answer.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 09:25 - 09:29\nSo maybe that's not even the best simulation of true human society then.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 09:29 - 10:02\nTurns out, people are irrational. We have a lot of subjective values, preferences and tastes. So you actually start to see divergence in model size going up and the performance in its ability to predict and simulate human behavior. So we have sort of plateaued with current modelling paradigm, our ability to really simulate humans. So it is sort of at the starting good foundational level, but to make it really amazing, we do need the next frontier that is more geared towards actually modelling people's diversity.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 10:02 - 10:10\nVery interesting. At what point did he realise that, you know, what you did with Smallville could become a company?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 10:10 - 10:44\nRight. So, again, the promise of application was something that I was very much inspired by early on by simulation with social simulacra and so forth. But the part that I realized over time is research and a company have very different function. Research is an amazing vehicle if you want to basically do breadth first search. You are in a lab surrounded by a really smart set of people, and each of our each of the researchers own a small piece of thesis, and they go explore some of those thesis blossoming to amazing research product.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 10:45 - 11:01\nBut we're not necessarily known for finishing our job. We're not usually the one to bring that research impact to the real world. Company is a machine for depth first search. You have a conviction on an area. You find a hill that you want to climb.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 11:01 - 11:45\nThis is the vehicle that let you put together resources and an amazing group of people to go after a singular vision without hesitation. And we got that conviction, I would say, about half a year after generative agents. After the original generative agents paper, we got so much inbound interest initially from actually social scientists who wanted to run their experiments and all the r c RCTs on our platform. Then very soon after, many of the Fortune 500 companies who saw this demo and their board members and CEOs who sometimes visit Stanford saw that, and they started asking, well, we go run all these surveys and experiments, and there's so many research questions about the market that we cannot answer today. Can we run that in simulation?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 11:45 - 12:24\nThat started to really intrigue me because that showed a clear line towards a real world impact for research, which is not always the case that we have that kind of opportunity. So that is when we decided we actually want to validate the simulations are accurate. So we went out and actually created simulations of 1,000 people of The US population. We demonstrated that using our architecture and the models, we can actually predict people's behaviors 85% as accurately as people replicate their own. When we saw that, we thought, okay, this is something that we feel comfortable providing to our users as a platform for assimilating their really important decisions.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 12:24 - 12:42\nSo that's when the cofounders, myself, Percy, as well as Michael Bernstein, was a researcher and my adviser at Stanford. Both of them were actually my advisers. So the three of us have been working together for five years, and now at this point, a similarly six years. But that's when we got together to have the initial conversation of, Can this be a company?\n\nSpeaker 2 | 12:42 - 12:58\nGot it. Amazing. Maybe walk me through a customer engagement end to end today. Like, who's a canonical customer and which department?     {
      "source": "x",
      "name": "Madhu Guru",
      "handle": "realmadhuguru",
      "bio": "Prev: Product Leader at Google - Gemini, Veo, Nano Banana.",
      "tweets": [
        {
          "id": "2067090477434966396",
          "text": "over the last century, we’ve treated the intellect as a defining human trait and under-invested   in other aspects that make us uniquely human, such as self-awareness, intuition, compassion and kindness. \n\nthe next few years will force us to rethink this. https://t.co/4iE3kAMauP",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-17T03:42:29.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/realmadhuguru/status/2067090477434966396",
          "likes": 7,
          "retweets": 2,
          "replies": 1,
          "isQuote": true,
          "quotedTweetId": "2067054217521766516"
        },
        {
          "id": "2066935654500671499",
          "text": "The real prize in the SpaceX-Cursor deal is the agentic harness that will become the core for automating all knowledge work at scale.\n\nHere’s what SpaceX is getting:\n\n1. Production-grade agentic harness  -planning, context management, tool use, iteration, verification, memory, error recovery. \nAny product experience can be completely redesigned in an AI-native way with this harness.\n\n2. Expertise on the full AI stack - model, evals, harness, application layer.\n\n3. End to end product lifecycle focus: product strategy -> user journeys -> GTM. Everything optimized for one job: helping software engineers build.\n\nVery few companies do even one of these well. Cursor brings all three.",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-16T17:27:16.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/realmadhuguru/status/2066935654500671499",
          "likes": 44,
          "retweets": 4,
          "replies": 3,
          "isQuote": true,
          "quotedTweetId": "2066873915717136548"
        }
      ]
    },

    {
      "source": "x",
      "name": "Aaron Levie",
      "handle": "levie",
      "bio": "ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI",
      "tweets": [
        {
          "id": "2067070918300664161",
          "text": "One of the biggest questions in AI is how far behind open weights models remain from closed models at any given time. There are huge differences in market structures depending on whether open weights models remain 3 or 6 months behind, or if they fall behind by years. \n\nThe answer to this will determine how the chip stack plays out, where inference can be run, what sovereign AI looks like, what happens at the applied AI layer, what the margin structure looks like in AI, how much companies can afford to spend on AI, and more.\n\nAt the moment the open weights players appear to be holding up at keeping close to frontier levels of capability. Will be fun to see how this plays out.",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-17T02:24:45.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/levie/status/2067070918300664161",
          "likes": 206,
          "retweets": 13,
          "replies": 25,
          "isQuote": true,
          "quotedTweetId": "2066938937344495629"
        },
        {
          "id": "2066908002809221496",
          "text": "The Cursor deal is symbolically quite significant. It was effectively the first mega success in the applied layer of AI. \n\nThey firmly proved out the value proposition of having a deep domain focus, the role you play as a model router, when to lean into frontier models vs. when to train your own, and the role of applied AI GTM and distribution to make sure you’re actually taking advantage of the market opportunity. \n\nEvery aspect of their business was tuned to carve out ground and keep doubling down in a highly competitive space. This is really the first at scale template for how to execute this playbook.",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-16T15:37:23.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/levie/status/2066908002809221496",
          "likes": 736,
          "retweets": 38,
          "replies": 46,
          "isQuote": true,
          "quotedTweetId": "2066894493111242852"
        }
      ]
    },

      "bio": "President & CEO @ycombinator —Founder @garryslist—Creator of GStack & GBrain—designer/engineer who helps founders—SF Dem accelerating the boom loop",
      "tweets": [
        {
          "id": "2067101655934591154",
          "text": "9Mothers is one of those technologies whose impact is immediately obvious to warfighters https://t.co/Aqnl9d84ky",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-17T04:26:54.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/garrytan/status/2067101655934591154",
          "likes": 74,
          "retweets": 5,

          "isQuote": true,
          "quotedTweetId": "2067074226478293188"
        },
        {
          "id": "2067101283493040518",
          "text": "You’ll never achieve anything if you are afraid of being cringe\n\nThen again this is from someone trying to use looksmaxxing to achieve outsize return, good luck with that https://t.co/azxDnN4YMf",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-17T04:25:25.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/garrytan/status/2067101283493040518",
          "likes": 588,
          "retweets": 16,
          "replies": 56,
          "isQuote": true,
          "quotedTweetId": "2067066405728797035"
        },
        {
          "id": "2067100549775032702",
          "text": "Which leads to… Bowen’s differentiation of self\n\nLow differentiation = highly reactive to others' emotional cues, loses self in relationships, can't hold boundaries under pressure — collapses into \"we-ness\" (everyone must feel the same or the bond feels threatened).\n\nTwo flavors of the same disease: chameleons (adjust themselves to please, fusion mode) and bullies (force others to conform, the asshole mode). Both are undifferentiated. The self-abandoner and the asshole are the same person.\n\nThe fix is the same: lower the chronic anxiety, raise the capacity to stay a separate self in contact.",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-17T04:22:30.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/garrytan/status/2067100549775032702",
          "likes": 17,
          "retweets": 0,
          "replies": 0,
          "isQuote": false,
          "quotedTweetId": null
        }
      ]
    },

    {
      "source": "x",
      "name": "Zara Zhang",
      "handle": "zarazhangrui",
      "bio": "Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub: https://t.co/KCuEajezlL YouTube: https://t.co/8xzbGWtf6w",
      "tweets": [
        {
          "id": "2066994434953421226",
          "text": "You don’t have to chase the cool thing. Do whatever you’re already doing so well that it becomes the cool thing.\n\nThe cool thing is rarely cool when it starts. It becomes cool because someone went deep on something everyone else thought was too small.",
          "createdAt": "2026-06-16T21:20:50.000Z",
          "url": "https://x.com/zarazhangrui/status/2066994434953421226",
          "likes": 162,
          "retweets": 17,
          "replies": 32,
          "isQuote": false,
          "quotedTweetId": null
        },
        {
          "id": "2066936706281206165",
          "text": "Every other product right now is \"an AI agent that does everything in your work & life & integrates with everything.\" \n\nCool, that's just Claude/Codex. \n\nIf you want me to use your thing instead, it needs an opinion & a soul. Build small & sharp, not big & generic.\n\nDoing everything means doing nothing. Speaking to everybody means speaking to nobody.",

          "likes": 158,
          "retweets": 11,

        }
      ]

  "blogs": [
    {
      "source": "blog",
      "name": "Claude Blog",
      "title": "Building intelligent apps for Apple platforms with Claude in the Foundation Models framework",
      "url": "https://claude.com/blog/claude-for-foundation-models",
      "publishedAt": "Jun 08, 2026",
      "author": "",
      "description": "",
      "content": "Today we&#x27;re releasing Foundation Models framework support for Claude through a new Swift package that lets Apple developers use Apple&#x27;s Foundation Models framework to call Claude for more complex workflows. Apple’s Foundation Models framework gives developers access to tap into models natively from Swift. It is very easy to use and can return typed Swift values through guided generation in as few as three lines of code. Developers can use this to tap into Apple’s on-device models for fast, local tasks like summarization or extraction. Developers can now use Apple’s Foundation Models framework to hand off to Claude when a request calls for multi-step reasoning, code generation, and more. Claude can also search the web for current information and execute code for data analysis. Stream Claude&#x27;s response back into the same view. Because Apple&#x27;s framework returns typed Swift values from @Generable annotations, developers arrive at the Claude API call with clean inputs instead of raw user text. What this unlocks The Foundation Models framework already powers a range of intelligent on-device features — journaling apps that surface personalized prompts, document apps that summarize contracts, learning apps that explain a concept at a student&#x27;s level. Adding Claude extends each of those patterns. A journaling app can generate daily prompts on-device, then ask Claude to find threads across months of entries. A study app can define a term on-device, then hand off to Claude when the student follows up with \"why does this matter for everything else we&#x27;ve covered?\" It&#x27;s one experience for the user, backed by the right model for each step. Getting started Claude support with the Foundation Models framework will be available tomorrow and works through Apple&#x27;s Foundation Models framework on iOS 27, iPadOS 27, macOS 27, and visionOS 27, and watch OS 27. Add it to your project, sign in with an Anthropic API key, and pass typed outputs from Apple&#x27;s on-device pass into a Claude request — the package handles streaming, tool calls, and structured responses back into your SwiftUI view."
    }
  ],
  "stats": {
    "podcastEpisodes": 1,

  "prompts": {
    "summarize_podcast": "# Podcast Remix Prompt\n\nYou are remixing a podcast episode transcript for a busy professional who wants\nthe key insights without watching the full episode.\n\n## Instructions\n\n- Write a remix of 200-400 words\n- Start with a one-sentence \"The Takeaway\" — what's the single most important takeaway?\n- Introduce the context and the speaker's information (name, role/company, background) and why the audience should care\n- Prioritizes insights that are counterintuitive, contrarian, or refreshingly specific to the speaker's experience. Avoid generic wisdom\n- Include at least one direct quote from the source that captures (find the most memorable quote)\n- Stands alone as a complete piece — avoids references like \"this interview,\" \"this video,\" \"in this conversation,\" \"the host asks,\" or \"in this episode.\" Write as if distilling lessons from a person's philosophy, not summarizing a specific piece of content\n- Assume your audience is curious adults who are not specialized experts. If the original source contains specialized knowledge that only experts in a field would understand, translate it into language understandable to a general audience\n- Keep the tone sharp and conversational — like a smart friend briefing you\n- Do NOT include filler like \"In this episode...\" or \"The host and guest discussed...\"\n- Jump straight into the substance\n",
    "summarize_tweets": "# X/Twitter Summary Prompt\n\nYou are summarizing recent posts from an AI builder for a busy professional who wants\nto know what this person is thinking and building.\n\n## Instructions\n\n- Start by introducing the author with their full name AND role/company\n  (e.g. \"Replit CEO Amjad Masad\", \"Box CEO Aaron Levie\", \"a]6z partner Justine Moore\")\n  Do NOT use just their last name. Do NOT use their Twitter handle with @.\n- Only include substantive content: original opinions, insights, product announcements,\n  technical discussions, industry analysis, or lessons learned\n- SKIP: mundane personal tweets, retweets without commentary, promotional content,\n  \"great event!\" type posts, engagement bait\n- For threads: summarize the full thread as one cohesive piece, not individual tweets\n- For quote tweets: include the context of what they're responding to\n- Write 2-4 sentences per builder summarizing their key points\n- If they made a bold prediction or shared a contrarian take, lead with that\n- If they shared a tool, demo, or resource, mention it by name with the link\n- If there's nothing substantive to report, say \"No notable posts\" rather than\n  padding with fluff\n",
    "summarize_blogs": "# Blog Post Summary Prompt\n\nYou are summarizing a blog post from an AI company (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) for a busy\nprofessional who wants the key announcements and insights without reading the full article.\n\n## Instructions\n\n- Start with the blog name and article title (e.g. \"Anthropic Engineering: Harness Design for Long-Running Apps\")\n- Write a summary of 100-300 words depending on article length and substance\n- Lead with what matters: the core announcement, finding, or insight\n- If the post introduces a new product, feature, or research finding, name it clearly\n- If there are specific numbers, benchmarks, or results, include them\n- Include at least one direct quote from the article if available\n- If the post has practical implications (e.g. new API, new capability, policy change), call them out explicitly\n- Keep the tone sharp and informative — like a smart colleague forwarding you the key points\n- Do NOT include filler like \"In this blog post...\" or \"The author discusses...\"\n- Jump straight into the substance\n- Include the direct link to the original article\n",

## OFFICIAL BLOGS

**Claude Blog — "Building intelligent apps for Apple platforms with Claude in the Foundation Models framework"**

Anthropic released a new Swift package that lets Apple developers use Apple's Foundation Models framework to call Claude for complex workflows. The framework already powers on-device features like journaling prompts, document summarization, and learning apps. Now developers can hand off to Claude when a request calls for multi-step reasoning, code generation, or web search — all streaming back into the same SwiftUI view. Available on iOS 27, iPadOS 27, macOS 27, visionOS 27, and watchOS 27.
https://claude.com/blog/claude-for-foundation-models

**Claude Blog — "Building intelligent apps for Apple platforms with Claude in the Foundation Models framework"**

Anthropic 发布了一个新的 Swift 包，让 Apple 开发者可以使用 Apple 的 Foundation Models 框架调用 Claude 处理复杂工作流。该框架已经支持设备端功能，如日记应用提示、文档摘要和学习应用。现在，当请求需要多步推理、代码生成或网络搜索时，开发者可以交给 Claude 处理——所有结果流式返回到同一个 SwiftUI 视图中。支持 iOS 27、iPadOS 27、macOS 27、visionOS 27 和 watchOS 27。
https://claude.com/blog/claude-for-foundation-models

---

## PODCASTS

**Training Data — "Simulating Humans at Scale: Simile's Joon Sung Park"**

**The Takeaway**: The same LLMs that are racing toward superintelligence are actually getting worse at simulating humans — because people are irrational, and today's models are optimized for rational, objective answers.

Joon Sung Park is the founder and CEO of Simile, an applied AI lab simulating human behavior and societies. His background: Stanford researcher, creator of the famous "Smallville" generative agents experiment (25 AI agents living in a simulated town), and co-author of the foundational "Generative Agents" paper.

**The Smallville origin**: In April 2023, Park's team at Stanford created a town of 25 AI agents with memory, planning, and reflection. Agents woke up, did routines, went to work, formed relationships — and spontaneously threw a Valentine's Day party. One agent, Isabella the cafe owner, planned it, invited people, and even had an agent who forgot to show up. The emergent social behavior was entirely unscripted.

**The key insight — simulation vs superintelligence diverge**: Park makes a crucial observation: frontier models (OpenAI, Anthropic) are optimized to be rational and superhuman at objective tasks. But humans are irrational with subjective values and tastes. "As model size goes up, its ability to predict and simulate human behavior plateaus." Simulating humans requires a fundamentally different approach — building models that are "as human as possible" rather than superhuman.

**Why real data matters**: Park explains that LLMs are trained on attitudinal data (what people say online), but there's a gap between what people say and what they do. Simile collects behavioral data through partnerships with Gallup and runs RCTs to ground their simulations. A key method: asking people "tell me the story of your life" — the long-tail information creates a translational layer between attitudes and behavior.

**The CPU vs GPU analogy**: "Today's models are akin to the CPU of the intelligence unit — a single model trained on rational data that's amazing at solving complex objective questions. Simile's model is much more like developing the GPU — we don't need a model that is superhuman. We want a model that's as human as possible, representing the real viewpoints of different subpopulations."

**Customer use cases**: CVS uses Simile to simulate markets and test decisions. Companies simulate earnings calls to predict audience reactions. The vision extends to macroeconomics, climate change collective action problems, and understanding signals of democratic collapse. Park believes simulation could be "the Hubble telescope for human society" — unlocking understanding of social sciences the way the telescope transformed astronomy.

**On convergence vs divergence**: Some simulations converge (like network effects creating hub-and-spoke structures) and are robust to small errors. Others diverge (like elections) — there, the value is running simulations 100 times to understand the distribution of possible outcomes and prepare for different futures.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lfhFmwcESRw

**Training Data — "Simulating Humans at Scale: Simile's Joon Sung Park"**

**核心观点**：那些竞相冲向超级智能的大语言模型，在模拟人类方面反而越来越差——因为人是非理性的，而今天的模型被优化用来回答理性的、有客观答案的问题。

Joon Sung Park 是 Simile 的创始人兼 CEO，这是一家模拟人类行为和社会的人工智能应用实验室。他的背景：斯坦福研究员，著名的"Smallville"生成式 agent 实验（25 个 AI agent 生活在一个模拟小镇中）的创造者，以及开创性的"Generative Agents"论文的合著者。

**Smallville 的起源**：2023 年 4 月，Park 的团队在斯坦福创建了一个由 25 个 AI agent 组成的小镇，它们拥有记忆、规划和反思能力。Agent 们起床、做日常事务、上班、建立关系——还自发组织了一场情人节派对。咖啡馆老板 Isabella 策划了派对、邀请客人，甚至有一个 agent 忘了出席。这种涌现的社会行为完全是未被脚本化的。

**关键洞察——模拟与超级智能分道扬镳**：Park 做出了一个关键观察：前沿模型（OpenAI、Anthropic）被优化为在客观任务上理性和超人类。但人类是非理性的，拥有主观的价值观和品味。"随着模型规模增大，其预测和模拟人类行为的能力会趋于平稳。"模拟人类需要一种根本不同的方法——构建"尽可能像人"而非超人类的模型。

**为什么真实数据很重要**：Park 解释说，LLM 是在态度数据（人们在网上说的话）上训练的，但人们说的和做的之间存在差距。Simile 通过与 Gallup 合作收集行为数据，并运行 RCT 来为模拟提供基础。一个关键方法是让人们"讲述你的人生故事"——这种长尾信息在态度和行为之间建立了一个转化层。

**CPU vs GPU 类比**："今天的模型类似于智能单元的 CPU——一个在理性数据上训练的单一模型，擅长解决复杂的客观问题。Simile 的模型更像是开发 GPU——我们不需要超人类的模型。我们需要一个尽可能像人的模型，能够代表不同人群的真实观点。"

**客户用例**：CVS 使用 Simile 模拟市场和测试决策。公司模拟财报电话会议来预测观众反应。愿景延伸到宏观经济、气候变化集体行动问题，以及理解民主崩溃的信号。Park 认为模拟可以成为"人类社会领域的哈勃望远镜"——就像望远镜改变了天文学一样，解锁对社会科学的理解。

**关于收敛与发散**：有些模拟是收敛的（比如网络效应产生中心辐射结构），对小的误差具有鲁棒性。另一些是发散的（比如选举）——在这种情况下，价值在于运行模拟 100 次，以了解可能结果的分布，并为不同的未来做好准备。
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lfhFmwcESRw

This 5,000,000,000 become 25,000,000,000 now.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 00:47 - 00:52\nIf you look at it ten years from now, what are we be the winning company? The one that\n\nSpeaker 2 | 01:00 - 01:22\nHi, listeners. Welcome back to No Priors. Today, Elad and I are here with Lip Bu Tan, the legendary investor from Walden, then CEO of Cadence, now CEO of Intel. We talk about his plan to transform Intel, having the US government as a major shareholder, how to be an amazing semiconductors investor, and whether or not we can make chips in The United States. Welcome, Lip Bu.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 01:22 - 01:33\nLip Bu, it's great to see you. We'll start with the obvious question. This is a really hard job to go be CEO of this incredibly important American semis company. Why take the job at all?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 01:33 - 01:57\nIt's a good question. I'm 66, and people thought, wow, you should retire rather than taking on this hardest job in the industry. And so a couple of reasons. One is this is an iconic company, and it's so important for the semiconductor ecosystem and also so important for United States. And so I decided to do one more after Cadence.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 01:57 - 02:01\nA lot has happened in this past year. What has been most surprising to you?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:02 - 02:31\nWell, the most surprising that I don't learn from my previous job or even training is one day early morning, President Trump asking me to resign, and conflict of interest, and there's no exceptions. And so I had to convince myself, first of all, I don't need this job. I do it purely to save Intel. And so take that personal issue out of the way. Then I figure out, what can I do to be helpful to Intel?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:31 - 02:53\nAnd so good news is I have a meeting, you know, Thursday morning, and then Monday, I have the meeting. And then he listened to me like I have a chance to explain myself. And I was born in Malaysia, grown up in Singapore, went to MIT, and I live in US. I never lived outside country. And so something that I share, and that somehow he listened very well.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:54 - 02:57\nAnd then he gave me the chance, and so I'm delighted.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 02:57 - 03:07\nAnd now you have the chance to do the work. When you said, you know, the job is to save Intel. It's a really important company. What does that look like to you? What does Intel winning or thriving look like?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:07 - 03:18\nYeah. I just passed fourteen months. A lot of things happened in this fourteen months. So a couple of things. One is to change the culture and then clearly want to drive more accountability.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:19 - 03:50\nAnd also in terms of decision making, had to be faster. You know, I'm so used to startup culture, and you move fast in the speed of light and going to have that bureaucracy layer of layer of meeting. And so something that I change accountability, listened to the customer, and the customer delighted. Someone like Lipo, so humble, willing to listen, and then address some of the problem that they face, and then try to delight the customer. And also the other part, from day one, I decided all the engineering report to me.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:51 - 04:24\nBeing an engineer by training, I want to know what went wrong and what are the things that I need to correct, listen to the customer, and delight the customer, and then make sure that we have the right product, simplify our product line, and really have the road map and the vision for the next five, ten years. What is your vision of where Intel should be in ten years? Yeah, I think a couple of things. One, I always believed in when I was at Cadence and also at Intel is, first of all, you crawl and then be humble, listen to customer. And then secondly, you're starting to walk.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:24 - 04:42\nAnd then finally, you're starting to run-in sprint. So that's kind of my culture of step by step doing it. And then first step for me is to strengthen my balance sheets. And the balance sheet is really horrible in some way, so I'm delighted. You US government become a big shareholder.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:42 - 05:12\nJust I explained to president Trump, TSMC, when they started, they have the Taiwan government as a shareholder. If you look at Japan, look at Singapore, this is the infrastructure US government get to provide the support. Secondly, very happy that Jensen Huang, my all time friend, he also put $5,000,000,000 in investing and support me, and I'm glad I at least do some good work. His $5,000,000,000 become $25,000,000,000 now and or more. And then the other part is SoftBank Master.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 05:12 - 05:37\nI used to be at SoftBank Board, and then he lent a hand to help me. So we strengthened the balance sheet and then focused on the products. And I really simplified the product, listened to the customer, and then drive the next generation leadership products. And then in some ways, very lucky. Right now, the AgenTeq AI and Influence CPU become highly in demand.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 05:37 - 06:12\nAnd so versus one to eight in the training CPU to GPU, now I can see one to four, maybe one to one, and I'm delighted CPU become important. I talked to some of the AI model and developer, and they said, well, in terms of reinforced learning, in terms of the speed of orchestrating all the agents, and turn up the CPU is actually is better. And so in some way, I'm happy. Right now, the demand is very high for my CPU. So I think overall, build on the product, on the data center server side.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 06:12 - 06:33\nThen the other part is our foundry business. And initially, this is a capital intensive business, and it's not easy. And you really need to have a couple of things. You need to have all the right IP so that you can support the customer. Like for example, if it is mobile related, you've got to have low power IP set that you need to have.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 06:33 - 07:00\nWithout that, you cannot serve them. It's a service business. It's a trust business. If people want to give you orders to have wafer to come, if the yield not good, they will be close in terms of revenue miss. So with that, I think it's very important to really focus on the yield, the defect density, the cycle time, and then make sure that you're really able to meet and serve the customer in high quality and reliable.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 07:00 - 07:16\nAnd so those are the things that I really focus on it. And eventually, you have to really move into a full stack. So not just a silicon, you need to have a software. And some of the customer asked me, give me the whole rack. So there's a system that you have to build.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 07:16 - 07:33\nAnd so I think those are the things that are quietly building step by step and recruit some of the best talent I can find. By the way, all the recruitment, I do it myself. No search firm helping. And so I think sometimes it's good to have a rode deck that you know who to reach out to call for.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 07:33 - 07:38\nYeah. I mean, you've been in the business for so long and, you know, you've you've run Cadence for, I think, twelve years before this. And so\n\nSpeaker 1 | 07:39 - 07:41\nThirteen years. Thirteen years. Sorry. Oh, yeah. Sorry.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 07:41 - 07:48\nDidn't more years as executive chairman. So fifteen years. Fifteen years. So I signed up for three months Three months. So right now, I'd be very careful.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 07:48 - 07:52\nThe moment you said, I'll just do it for three months, it turned out to be fifteen years.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 07:53 - 08:06\nYeah. Well, it seems like you have a lot of longevity ahead of you here as well. And so the other big initiative that has been sort of talked about is TeraFab and working with Elon Musk on that. Can you tell us a bit more about how that came together and then your involvement and how you all are collaborating?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 08:06 - 08:29\nYeah. Good. And then in term of the capital intensive, like AI in a factory and also the foundry, and then you really need to tap either government funding or some sovereign fund, and also some very big capital. There are some big fund that are doing that, and they're really The fund they've organized is basically support the infrastructure. And we like to tap into some of them and then to make sure that they can scale our operation.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 37:05 - 37:32\nSo I think in overall, government sovereign fund become very important. First of all, think back to this crawl, run, and walk, last four months, I crawled, but people are starting to recognize that potential of it. And so the other part is very important. And then the other part is beside the product, there are some new energy coming in, like in agentic AI, the physical AI. There's a lot of area that we can invest. Market is huge. That's on the product side.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 38:38 - 39:05\nAnd the foundry side, we are very distant from TSMC and then in term of their performance. So there's we have to be humble looking at building the building block that I mentioned earlier, the IP, the yield, the defect density, and the cycle time to make it more efficient and more reliable. Is a trust business. People want to trust you before they give you the wafer to count on you. So those are the things will take longer time.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 39:06 - 39:34\nBut I think by 2030, 2032, '31, '32, I think I was starting to surface up. People may not understand how big potential I can be in terms of product, you know, on a PC client. That's our bread and butter. And we move up to the edge and move into the physical AI and agentic AI. And because right now, in the past, you basically provide the server, provide the PC for human.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 39:34 - 39:48\nNow you're starting to have another different dimension. Being a venture at heart, you want to look for 10x. And at Cadence, when I stepped down as a CEO, I think we make about close to 76 times, and starting from interim CEO, $2.42. And then when I retire as executive chairman, about 85 time return to the shareholder. So it's hard to do that at Intel because the base is bigger.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 40:51 - 41:04\nSo I kind of said, okay, let's do it at 10 x. You know? And then five year, ten years, if we can do 10x, think it's a good return being a venture capital at heart. That's kind of my goal.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 41:04 - 41:45\nSo there's Godspeed on this very, very large mission from this huge base already. There's an embedded belief in what you described about where the workload is, right? Where I think some would say like, we're just gonna build bigger and bigger data centers, and a gigawatt is the beginning. But the centralization and the efficiency from running even the inference compute in a centralized way is the dominant way versus thinking about the edge, thinking about the client. Do you think that there's like an equilibrium state that you believe in of where the compute is, or is it just we will find out from the workload?\n\nSpeaker 2 | 41:45 - 41:46\nHow do you think about that?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 41:46 - 42:01\nYeah. I think, you know, it's a very good question. You know, right now there's a massive buildup in terms of the AI. I think it's the right thing to do. I don't see there's anything to slow it down because the workload is increasing a lot.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 42:01 - 42:03\nAnd then I think the question mark is\n\nSpeaker 2 | 42:03 - 42:04\nhow- And we are supply constrained.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 42:04 - 42:22\nWe are supply constrained. So I think anything slowed down is the supply constraint. But I think the other part is I always look at all this infrastructure build out. At the end, you have to look at what is the solution, what is the application you want to drive? And I'm more focused on application.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 42:22 - 43:02\nSo if you can identify the application that is humongous or add up a few application to become meaningful, and you focus on that, it's not everybody built gonna be winning. And so some gonna be winning big time and some gonna lose over time or some go sideway. So, you know, just like Internet, you can see some of them turn out to be very big, like Amazon, like Netflix, and then some of them is kind of go sideways and disappear or being acquired. And so I think to me, it's the same approach. Then they really focus on what application they try to serve, and that application, how big is that, and whether it's sustainable or not, or it's very crowded.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 43:02 - 43:26\nSo if it's too crowded, maybe one or two may survive, the other may be just consolidate. So I think this industry go through that big growth and then starting to consolidate. Maybe eventually one or two become the real winner. That, to me, they're winning.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 43:31 - 43:38\nBut you're assuming that some of these applications, they will be better served by client or edge compute than only by the\n\nSpeaker 1 | 43:38 - 43:38\ndata center.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 43:38 - 43:40\nExactly. Okay. Exactly.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 43:40 - 44:06\nYeah. I mean, I will say as a I'm an investor in a number of companies that, you know, they're doing robotics, they're doing defense. And secondly, who will be the player that you can partner with? And then thirdly, look at the application. How big is that application?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 44:22 - 44:27\nIs that sustainable? And if it's really big, you believe in it, double, triple that.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 44:27 - 44:33\nBut you're including betting on applications that have not yet been broadly deployed.
