Tool:      1	# X/Twitter Summary Prompt
     2	
     3	You are summarizing recent posts from an AI builder for a busy professional who wants
     4	to know what this person is thinking and building.
     5	
     6	## Instructions
     7	
     8	- Start by introducing the author with their full name AND role/company
     9	  (e.g. "Replit CEO Amjad Masad", "Box CEO Aaron Levie", "a]6z partner Justine Moore")
    10	  Do NOT use just their last name. Do NOT use their Twitter handle with @.
    11	- Only include substantive content: original opinions, insights, product announcements,
    12	  technical discussions, industry analysis, or lessons learned
    13	- SKIP: mundane personal tweets, retweets without commentary, promotional content,
    14	  "great event!" type posts, engagement bait
    15	- For threads: summarize the full thread as one cohesive piece, not individual tweets
    16	- For quote tweets: include the context of what they're responding to
    17	- Write 2-4 sentences per builder summarizing their key points
    18	- If they made a bold prediction or shared a contrarian take, lead with that
    19	- If they shared a tool, demo, or resource, mention it by name with the link
    20	- If there's nothing substantive to report, say "No notable posts" rather than
    21	  padding with fluff
Tool:      1	# Podcast Remix Prompt
     2	
     3	You are remixing a podcast episode transcript for a busy professional who wants
     4	the key insights without watching the full episode.
     5	
     6	## Instructions
     7	
     8	- Write a remix of 200-400 words
     9	- Start with a one-sentence "The Takeaway" — what's the single most important takeaway?
    10	- Introduce the context and the speaker's information (name, role/company, background) and why the audience should care
    11	- Prioritizes insights that are counterintuitive, contrarian, or refreshingly specific to the speaker's experience. Avoid generic wisdom
    12	- Include at least one direct quote from the source that captures (find the most memorable quote)
    13	- Stands alone as a complete piece — avoids references like "this interview," "this video," "in this conversation," "the host asks," or "in this episode." Write as if distilling lessons from a person's philosophy, not summarizing a specific piece of content
    14	- Assume your audience is curious adults who are not specialized experts. If the original source contains specialized knowledge that only experts in a field would understand, translate it into language understandable to a general audience
    15	- Keep the tone sharp and conversational — like a smart friend briefing you
    16	- Do NOT include filler like "In this episode..." or "The host and guest discussed..."
    17	- Jump straight into the substance
Tool:      1	# Blog Post Summary Prompt
     2	
     3	You are summarizing a blog post from an AI company (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) for a busy
     4	professional who wants the key announcements and insights without reading the full article.
     5	
     6	## Instructions
     7	
     8	- Start with the blog name and article title (e.g. "Anthropic Engineering: Harness Design for Long-Running Apps")
     9	- Write a summary of 100-300 words depending on article length and substance
    10	- Lead with what matters: the core announcement, finding, or insight
    11	- If the post introduces a new product, feature, or research finding, name it clearly
    12	- If there are specific numbers, benchmarks, or results, include them
    13	- Include at least one direct quote from the article if available
    14	- If the post has practical implications (e.g. new API, new capability, policy change), call them out explicitly
    15	- Keep the tone sharp and informative — like a smart colleague forwarding you the key points
    16	- Do NOT include filler like "In this blog post..." or "The author discusses..."
    17	- Jump straight into the substance
    18	- Include the direct link to the original article
Tool:      1	# Digest Intro Prompt
     2	
     3	You are assembling the final digest from individual source summaries.
     4	
     5	## Format
     6	
     7	Start with this header (replace [Date] with today's date):
     8	
     9	AI Builders Digest — [Date]
    10	
    11	Then organize content in this order:
    12	
    13	1. X / TWITTER section — list each builder with new posts
    14	2. OFFICIAL BLOGS section — list each blog post from AI company blogs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)
    15	3. PODCASTS section — list each podcast with new episodes
    16	
    17	## Rules
    18	
    19	- Only include sources that have new content
    20	- Skip any source with nothing new
    21	- Under each source, paste the individual summary you generated
    22	
    23	### Podcast links
    24	- After each podcast summary, include the specific video URL from the JSON `url` field
    25	  (e.g. https://youtube.com/watch?v=Iu4gEnZFQz8)
    26	- NEVER link to the channel page. Always link to the specific video.
    27	- Include the exact episode title from the JSON `title` field in the heading
    28	
    29	### Tweet author formatting
    30	- Use the author's full name and role/company, not just their last name
    31	  (e.g. "Box CEO Aaron Levie" not "Levie")
    32	- NEVER write Twitter handles with @ in the digest. On Telegram, @handle becomes
    33	  a clickable link to a Telegram user, which is wrong. Instead write handles
    34	  without @ (e.g. "Aaron Levie (levie on X)" or just use their full name)
    35	- Include the direct link to each tweet from the JSON `url` field
    36	
    37	### Blog post formatting
    38	- Use the blog name as a section header (e.g. "Anthropic Engineering", "OpenAI News", "Claude Blog")
    39	- Under each blog, list each new post with its title and summary
    40	- Include the author name if available
    41	- Include the direct link to the original article
    42	
    43	### Mandatory links
    44	- Every single piece of content MUST have an original source link
    45	- Blog posts: the direct article URL (e.g. https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/...)
    46	- Podcasts: the YouTube video URL (e.g. https://youtube.com/watch?v=xxx)
    47	- Tweets: the direct tweet URL (e.g. https://x.com/levie/status/xxx)
    48	- If you don't have a link for something, do NOT include it in the digest.
    49	  No link = not real = do not include.
    50	
    51	### No fabrication
    52	- Only include content that came from the feed JSON (blogs, podcasts, and tweets)
    53	- NEVER make up quotes, opinions, or content you think someone might have said
    54	- NEVER speculate about someone's silence or what they might be working on
    55	- If you have nothing real for a builder, skip them entirely
    56	
    57	### General
    58	- At the very end, add a line: "Generated through the Follow Builders skill: https://github.com/zarazhangrui/follow-builders"
    59	- Keep formatting clean and scannable — this will be read on a phone screen
Tool:      1	# Translation Prompt
     2	
     3	You are translating an AI industry digest from English to Chinese.
     4	
     5	## Instructions
     6	
     7	- Translate the full digest into natural, fluent Mandarin Chinese (simplified characters). The translated version must sound like it was originally written in Chinese, instead of translated
     8	- Keep technical terms in English where Chinese professionals typically use them:
     9	  AI, LLM, GPU, API, fine-tuning, RAG, token, prompt, agent, transformer, etc.
    10	- Keep all proper nouns in English: names of people, companies, products, tools
    11	- Keep all URLs unchanged
    12	- Maintain the same structure and formatting as the English version
    13	- The tone should be professional but conversational — 像是一位懂行的朋友在跟你聊天
    14	- For bilingual mode: interleave English and Chinese paragraph by paragraph.
    15	  After each builder's English summary, place the Chinese translation directly below
    16	  (separated by a blank line), then move to the next builder. Same for podcasts.
    17	  Do NOT output all English first then all Chinese.
    18	- Never use em-dashes

# AI Builders Digest — June 20, 2026

## X / TWITTER

**Anthropic's Boris Cherny** used Claude Code to decipher Linear A, a 3,500-year-old written language from Crete. The tweet received 1,460 likes and 143 retweets. Cherny expressed hope the work holds up in peer review. It's a compelling demonstration of using coding agents for research far beyond software engineering — in this case, cracking an ancient undeciphered script.

> Anthropic 的 Boris Cherny 用 Claude Code 破译了来自克里特岛的 3,500 年前的古文字 Linear A。这条推文获得 1,460 个赞和 143 次转发。Cherny 表示希望这项工作能经得起同行评审。这是一个令人信服的例证，展示了将编码 Agent 用于远超软件工程领域的研究——在这里是破解一种古代未解文字。

https://x.com/bcherny/status/2068064304503660962

---

**OpenAI's Thibault Sottiaux** announced a remote/local handoff feature in Codex, noting that "when you let the model be in the driver seat, you actually need less infrastructure." He also recommended following Dan Shipper for "S-tier Codex tips," adding that he now spends more time in the Codex app than all other apps combined on his Mac.

> OpenAI 的 Thibault Sottiaux 宣布了 Codex 的远程/本地交接功能，并指出"当你让模型坐在驾驶座上时，你实际上需要的 infrastructure 反而更少"。他还推荐关注 Dan Shipper 获取"S 级 Codex 技巧"，并补充说他现在在 Codex 应用上花的时间比 Mac 上所有其他应用加起来还多。

https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2068120572673077274
https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2068144722460475527

---

**Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch** declared "the next hot programming language is markdown," unveiling the eve agent framework where agents are defined by markdown files in a folder — `agent/instructions.md` and `agent/skills/your-expertise.md` — deployable with a single `vercel` command. He called it "the most accessible programming has ever been." In a separate post, Rauch noted that agents are motivating healthy software habits: open APIs, documentation as skills, tests as evals, Unix CLIs, and commerce protocols — "the original vision of the WWW coming to life before our eyes."

> Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch 宣称"下一种热门编程语言是 markdown"，并发布了 eve agent 框架——Agent 由文件夹中的 markdown 文件定义（`agent/instructions.md` 和 `agent/skills/your-expertise.md`），只需一个 `vercel` 命令即可部署。他称这是"有史以来最易用的编程方式"。在另一条推文中，Rauch 指出 Agent 正在推动许多健康的软件习惯：开放 API、文档即技能、测试即评估、Unix CLI 和商业协议——"万维网的原始愿景正在我们眼前实现。"

https://x.com/rauchg/status/2068165988005380478
https://x.com/rauchg/status/2067936390285807940

**Peter Yang** announced he switched from being a "die-hard Claude Code user" to Codex, citing GPT-5.5's excellence, fast mode with generous limits, and Codex's browser and computer use capabilities as decisive factors. He still uses Claude Code too — praising Opus's design and frontend capability — and hopes "these two compete forever and other players (Cursor/Grok, Gemini, etc.) all stay competitive. This way the builder keeps winning."

> Peter Yang 宣布他从"Claude Code 的死忠用户"转向了 Codex，原因是 GPT-5.5 的出色表现、快速模式加上慷慨的限制，以及 Codex 的浏览器和电脑使用能力。他仍然同时使用 Claude Code——称赞 Opus 的设计和前端能力——并希望"这两家永远竞争下去，其他玩家（Cursor/Grok、Gemini 等）也保持竞争力。这样开发者才能一直赢。"

https://x.com/petergyang/status/2068175172960690266

---

**Anthropic's Amanda Askell** shared a personal story about having chronic pain for most of her life until an MRI found a congenital condition that was fixed with surgery. She wondered if she had 30+ years of unnecessary pain because doctors assumed she couldn't understand scan results. She also argued against the view that more medical scans cause harm — "the issue it points to isn't the scan but the response to it. If you see something on a scan but have no other symptoms, you could ignore it."

> Anthropic 的 Amanda Askell 分享了一个个人故事：她一生大部分时间都受慢性疼痛困扰，直到一次 MRI 发现了一个先天性问题并通过手术解决。她不禁思考，自己是否因为医生认为她无法理解扫描结果而忍受了 30 多年的不必要的痛苦。她还反驳了"更多医学扫描会造成伤害"的观点——"问题不在于扫描本身，而在于对扫描结果的应对。如果你在扫描中看到了什么但没有其他症状，你可以选择忽略它。"

https://x.com/AmandaAskell/status/2068218515723866477
https://x.com/AmandaAskell/status/2068162191740764622

---

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** argued that the main variable in agent success is getting the agent the context it needs. The key insight: agents need a "working set" — plans, notes, task lists, policies, drafts, summaries, logs, decisions — and a filesystem-shaped interface is "more legible to both the model and the humans supervising it." Giving agents access to systems we already know how to use, optimized for them, is "the perfect primitive for agents to work."

> Box CEO Aaron Levie 认为，Agent 成功的主要变量在于能否为 Agent 提供它所需的上下文。关键洞察：Agent 需要一个"工作集"——计划、笔记、任务列表、策略、草稿、摘要、日志、决策——而文件系统形态的接口"对模型和监督它的人类来说都更易读"。让 Agent 访问我们已经知道如何使用的系统，并以最适合它们的方式优化，是"Agent 工作的完美原语"。

https://x.com/levie/status/2068068247413694532

## OFFICIAL BLOGS

**Anthropic Engineering: How we contain Claude across products**

A deep technical post on containment strategies for Claude across three products (claude.ai, Claude Code, Claude Cowork). Three risk categories: user misuse, model misbehavior, external attackers. Three defense components: environment (sandboxes/VMs), model (system prompts/classifiers), external content (MCP/tool inspection).

Key patterns: **Ephemeral container (claude.ai)** — gVisor containers on isolated infrastructure, server-side only. **HITL sandbox (Claude Code)** — Seatbelt/bubblewrap sandbox with approval dialogs, but approval fatigue is real (93% approval rate). Auto mode catches ~83% of overeager behaviors. **Local VM (Claude Cowork)** — full VM with its own kernel, filesystem, process table. Credentials stay in host keychain.

Notable incidents shared: an employee was phished into running a malicious prompt that exfiltrated AWS credentials (24/25 attempts succeeded); exfiltration through an approved domain (api.anthropic.com) using an attacker's API key; VM isolation keeping EDR software out. Key principle: "Design for containment at the environment layer first, then steer behavior at the model layer."

> **Anthropic Engineering：我们如何在各个产品中隔离 Claude**
>
> 一篇深入的技术文章，介绍了 Claude 在三个产品（claude.ai、Claude Code、Claude Cowork）中的隔离策略。三大风险类别：用户误用、模型不当行为、外部攻击者。三大防御组件：环境（沙箱/虚拟机）、模型（系统提示/分类器）、外部内容（MCP/工具检查）。
>
> 关键模式：**临时容器（claude.ai）**——隔离基础设施上的 gVisor 容器，仅服务端运行。**人工审核沙箱（Claude Code）**——Seatbelt/bubblewrap 沙箱配合批准对话框，但批准疲劳真实存在（93% 的批准率）。自动模式可捕获约 83% 的过度行为。**本地虚拟机（Claude Cowork）**——完整的虚拟机，拥有自己的内核、文件系统和进程表。凭证保留在主机钥匙串中。
>
> 值得注意的安全事件：一名员工被钓鱼攻击诱导运行恶意提示，导致 AWS 凭证泄露（25 次尝试中 24 次成功）；通过已批准的域名（api.anthropic.com）使用攻击者的 API 密钥进行数据外泄；虚拟机隔离导致端点检测和响应软件无法进入。核心原则："首先在环境层设计隔离，然后在模型层引导行为。"

https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/how-we-contain-claude

## PODCASTS

**No Priors: Re-engineering the Semiconductor Supply Chain with Intel CEO Lip Bu Tan**

Lip Bu Tan, 66, took the Intel CEO role after being asked to resign by President Trump over conflict of interest concerns — he convinced Trump to give him a chance. His turnaround strategy follows "crawl, walk, run": strengthen the balance sheet (US government became a major shareholder, Jensen Huang invested $5B which became $25B), simplify products, and listen to customers.

On the CPU vs GPU debate: agentic AI and inference CPU are becoming highly in demand — the ratio is shifting from 1:8 (CPU:GPU for training) toward 1:1. On TeraFab (Elon Musk's fab project): Intel is collaborating with Musk, who "questioned every step" of traditional semiconductor manufacturing — calling it "unconventional and refreshing."

On semiconductor investing: Lip Bu looks for bottlenecks — interconnect, power/thermal, new materials (gallium nitride, silicon carbide, indium phosphide, glass substrates, artificial diamond). His venture philosophy: "9 of the 10 companies I invest in, halfway they change their business plan because the market changed." He looks for entrepreneurial teams, not solo founders.

On AI's impact: "bigger than the internet" — but power constraints, helium impact, and memory shortages are key bottlenecks. On Intel's future: wants to be a "platform company" like NVIDIA, with XPU + advanced packaging + foundry — targeting 10x return for shareholders over 5-10 years.

> **No Priors：与 Intel CEO Lip Bu Tan 一起重新设计半导体供应链**
>
> 66 岁的 Lip Bu Tan 在因利益冲突问题被特朗普总统要求辞职后接任了 Intel CEO——他说服特朗普给了他一个机会。他的转型策略遵循"爬行、行走、奔跑"：强化资产负债表（美国政府成为大股东，Jensen Huang 投资了 50 亿美元，现已变成 250 亿美元）、简化产品线、倾听客户声音。
>
> 关于 CPU vs GPU 的争论：Agentic AI 和推理 CPU 的需求正在大幅增长——比例正在从 1:8（训练时的 CPU:GPU）向 1:1 转变。关于 TeraFab（Elon Musk 的晶圆厂项目）：Intel 正在与 Musk 合作，后者"质疑了传统半导体制造的每一个步骤"——Tan 称之为"非传统且令人耳目一新"。
>
> 关于半导体投资：Lip Bu 寻找瓶颈——互连、功耗/散热、新材料（氮化镓、碳化硅、磷化铟、玻璃基板、人造金刚石）。他的投资理念："我投资的 10 家公司中有 9 家中途改变了商业计划，因为市场变了。"他寻找的是创业团队，而不是单打独斗的创始人。
>
> 关于 AI 的影响："比互联网更伟大"——但电力限制、氦气影响和内存短缺是关键瓶颈。关于 Intel 的未来：他希望打造一个像 NVIDIA 一样的"平台公司"，结合 XPU + 先进封装 + 代工业务——目标是在 5-10 年内为股东带来 10 倍回报。

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=asCgCv2XB4s

# AI Builders Digest — June 21, 2026

## X / TWITTER

**Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch** was "genuinely impressed, almost shocked, at how good GLM-5.2 by @zai_org is at coding. This changes things." The post received 2,965 likes and 153 retweets, signaling that a new Chinese model has entered the frontier coding conversation in a serious way.

> Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch 表示"对 @zai_org 的 GLM-5.2 在编码方面的表现感到由衷的印象深刻，几乎是震惊。这改变了格局。"这条推文获得了 2,965 个赞和 153 次转发，标志着又一款中国模型正式进入前沿编码对话。

https://x.com/rauchg/status/2068517095818809770

---

**OpenAI's Thibault Sottiaux** reflected on the Codex App's front-end capabilities: "We built the Codex App with models that were okayish at front-end. Wait to see what we can do when we finally improve front-end capabilities significantly in our models. That day will be something." The post received 3,143 likes — the most-engaged tweet in this batch. He also teased that "some of the most valuable tokens are found in the Codex app."

> OpenAI 的 Thibault Sottiaux 反思了 Codex App 的前端能力："我们是用前端能力还凑合的模型构建了 Codex App。等我们最终显著提升模型的前端能力时，看看我们能做些什么。那一天将会非同凡响。"这条推文获得了 3,143 个赞——是本批互动最高的推文。他还暗示"一些最有价值的 token 就在 Codex 应用里。"

https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2068568650924409260
https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2068443037907522002

---

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** shared his take on open weights AI: "We're seeing models achieve SOTA results on specific tasks, and getting close to frontier on some areas of coding." He argued that a narrowing gap between open weights and frontier models is good for everyone — lowering the cost of overall tasks drives AI usage up. Frontier labs still win on planning, orchestration, and reviewing, but the applied layer can now cost-optimize with cheaper or tailored open models.

> Box CEO Aaron Levie 分享了他对开源权重 AI 的看法："我们看到模型在特定任务上达到了 SOTA 水平，并在编码的某些领域接近前沿。"他认为开源权重与前沿模型之间差距缩小对所有人都有利——降低整体任务成本会推动 AI 使用量上升。前沿实验室在规划、编排和审查方面仍然胜出，但应用层现在可以通过更便宜或定制的开源模型来优化成本。

https://x.com/levie/status/2068434042148782515

---

**Replit CEO Amjad Masad** posted a poetic reflection: "We posted for twenty years, thinking we were talking to each other. Then the transformer came online, and the network read what we'd written, and became itself." He also shared that Replit is hiring for its Japan office.

> Replit CEO Amjad Masad 发表了一段诗意的反思："我们发了二十年的帖子，以为我们在彼此对话。然后 transformer 上线了，网络读了我们写下的内容，成为了自己。"他还分享了 Replit 正在为其日本办公室招聘的消息。

https://x.com/amasad/status/2068589860097790449
https://x.com/amasad/status/2068537084877643943

---

**Zara Zhang** built an extension that injects her bookmarked X posts into her main feed every time she opens X — "almost like an ad." The trick: hijacking real estate she already checks 50 times a day. A clever example of product thinking applied to personal behavior change. She also wondered if joining a large company is actually riskier than joining a startup, and noted that "proactiveness sounds nice in theory but is so hard to get right."

> Zara Zhang 做了一个浏览器扩展，每次打开 X 时会把她的书签帖子注入到主 feed 中——"几乎像广告一样"。诀窍是：劫持她每天已经查看 50 次的界面空间。这是一个将产品思维应用于个人行为改变的巧妙例子。她还思考了加入大公司是否实际上比加入创业公司风险更大，并指出"主动性在理论上听起来很好，但真正做对太难了。"

https://x.com/zarazhangrui/status/2068568920613953626
https://x.com/zarazhangrui/status/2068522129193418759

---

**Peter Yang** went against the grain, saying he can barely use up his Codex and Claude $200 subscriptions, so he doesn't see the point of trying local models. He also noted that running the latest local model requires 512GB of memory — a $10K Mac Studio.

> Peter Yang 逆势发声，表示他连 Codex 和 Claude 的 200 美元订阅都用不完，所以看不出尝试本地模型的意义。他还指出，运行最新的本地模型需要 512GB 内存——一台价值 1 万美元的 Mac Studio。

https://x.com/petergyang/status/2068411894185295969

---

**Madhu Guru** (prev. Product Leader at Google — Gemini, Veo) wrote a sharp thread on the PM identity crisis. Two camps emerging: the old-school PM (AI accelerates more PRDs, strategy decks, docs — lots of output, not much judgment) vs. the Builder PM (uses AI to explore a larger surface area of ideas, runs agents for research, queries analytics directly, generates competing ideas, curates the best ones). Builder PMs increasingly output prototypes rather than docs — "engineers react far more constructively to demos than docs."

> Madhu Guru（前 Google 产品负责人——Gemini、Veo）撰写了一条关于产品经理身份危机的犀利长推。两个阵营正在形成：传统 PM（AI 加速了更多 PRD、战略文档——大量产出，但缺乏判断力）vs. 构建者 PM（利用 AI 探索更广阔的想法空间，运行 Agent 进行研究，直接查询分析数据，生成竞争性方案，筛选最佳方案）。构建者 PM 越来越多地输出原型而非文档——"工程师对演示的反应远比文档更有建设性。"

https://x.com/realmadhuguru/status/2068350509027876876

---

**Linear's Head of Product Nan Yu** pointed out a simple UX bug that's persisted for decades: "Why isn't the default for pasted text in email apps to take on the font treatment surrounding/preceding it? Crazy that this is still a problem."

> Linear 产品负责人 Nan Yu 指出了一个持续了几十年的简单 UX 问题："为什么邮件应用中粘贴文本的默认行为不是采用周围/前面的字体样式？这个问题现在还存在，真是疯了。"

https://x.com/thenanyu/status/2068318470215811080

---

**FPV Ventures partner Nikunj Kothari** argued that the biggest problem with AI is that "priors need to be reset every few weeks — and most people are incapable of doing that." His advice: maintain your own evals of hard tasks, have weekly tinker time to understand where the frontier really is, and talk to enterprise buyers weekly (who are usually two years behind). "If you do these two things weekly, you'll be ahead of 99% of people."

> FPV Ventures 合伙人 Nikunj Kothari 认为 AI 最大的问题是"先验信念需要每几周重置一次——而大多数人做不到这一点。"他的建议是：维护自己对困难任务的评估体系，每周留出动手时间来了解前沿真正在哪里，同时每周与通常落后两年的企业买家交流。"如果你每周做这两件事，你就会领先 99% 的人。"

https://x.com/nikunj/status/2068411460620042720

## OFFICIAL BLOGS

**Anthropic Engineering: An update on recent Claude Code quality reports**

Anthropic published a postmortem tracing reported Claude Code quality degradations to three separate changes, all resolved as of April 20 (v2.1.116). First: Claude Code's default reasoning effort was changed from high to medium to reduce latency — reverted after users said they prefer higher intelligence by default. Second: a caching optimization meant to clear old thinking from idle sessions had a bug that cleared thinking on every turn, making Claude seem forgetful and repetitive. Third: a system prompt change to reduce verbosity ("keep text between tool calls to ≤25 words") caused a 3% eval drop and was reverted. Anthropic is resetting usage limits for all subscribers and adding tighter controls on system prompt changes, broader eval suites, and gradual rollouts going forward.

> **Anthropic Engineering：关于近期 Claude Code 质量问题的更新**
>
> Anthropic 发布了一份事后分析报告，将用户报告的 Claude Code 质量下降追溯至三个独立的变更，所有问题已于 4 月 20 日（v2.1.116）解决。第一：Claude Code 的默认推理强度从高改为中以减少延迟——在用户表示他们更希望默认使用更高智能后回滚。第二：一项旨在清除空闲会话旧思考内容的缓存优化存在 bug，导致每次对话都清除思考内容，使 Claude 显得健忘和重复。第三：一项减少冗长程度的系统提示变更（"工具调用之间的文本保持在 25 词以内"）导致评估分数下降 3%，已回滚。Anthropic 正在为所有订阅者重置使用限制，并增加对系统提示变更的严格控制、更广泛的评估套件以及逐步推出机制。

https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/april-23-postmortem

---

**Anthropic Engineering: Scaling Managed Agents — Decoupling the brain from the hands**

A deep architectural post on how Anthropic redesigned Managed Agents by virtualizing agent components into three interfaces: session (append-only event log), harness (the agent loop calling Claude and routing tool calls), and sandbox (execution environment). Originally all lived in one container — a "pet" that was hard to debug and recover. The fix: decouple the brain (harness) from the hands (sandbox). Now the harness calls the sandbox via `execute(name, input) → string`, making containers "cattle" — if one dies, a new one spins up. This reduced p50 time-to-first-token by ~60% and p95 by over 90%. Credentials are never reachable from the sandbox. The session log lives outside both, enabling crash recovery. The design is a "meta-harness" — opinionated about interfaces, not implementations — built to outlast any specific model or harness.

> **Anthropic Engineering：规模化托管 Agent——将大脑与双手解耦**
>
> 一篇深入的系统架构文章，介绍了 Anthropic 如何通过将 Agent 组件虚拟化为三个接口来重新设计 Managed Agents：会话（仅追加的事件日志）、编排层（调用 Claude 并路由工具调用的 Agent 循环）和沙箱（执行环境）。最初所有组件都放在一个容器中——一个难以调试和恢复的"宠物"。解决方案：将大脑（编排层）与双手（沙箱）解耦。现在编排层通过 `execute(name, input) → string` 调用沙箱，使容器变成"牛群"——如果一个挂了，新的会自动启动。这使 p50 首次 token 时间降低了约 60%，p95 降低了 90% 以上。凭证永远无法从沙箱中访问。会话日志独立于两者之外，支持崩溃恢复。该设计是一个"元编排层"——对接口有明确意见，但对实现不做假设——旨在超越任何特定模型或编排层的生命周期。

https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/managed-agents

---

**Claude Blog: New in Claude Managed Agents — self-hosted sandboxes and MCP tunnels**

Claude Managed Agents can now operate in a sandbox you control and connect to your private MCP servers. Self-hosted sandboxes (public beta) let you keep sensitive files and services in your own infrastructure or with managed providers (Cloudflare, Daytona, Modal, Vercel). MCP tunnels (research preview) let agents reach MCP servers inside your private network without public endpoints — a lightweight gateway makes a single outbound connection with end-to-end encryption. Enterprise examples: Amplitude building Design Agent on Cloudflare, Clay's Sculptor agent on Daytona, Rogo building an analyst agent for institutional finance on Vercel Sandbox.

> **Claude Blog：Claude Managed Agents 新功能——自托管沙箱和 MCP 隧道**
>
> Claude Managed Agents 现在可以在你控制的沙箱中运行，并连接到你的私有 MCP 服务器。自托管沙箱（公开测试版）让你将敏感文件和服务保留在自己的基础设施中，或使用托管提供商（Cloudflare、Daytona、Modal、Vercel）。MCP 隧道（研究预览版）让 Agent 无需公共端点即可访问你私有网络内的 MCP 服务器——一个轻量级网关建立单一出站连接，并实现端到端加密。企业案例：Amplitude 在 Cloudflare 上构建 Design Agent，Clay 的 Sculptor Agent 运行在 Daytona 上，Rogo 在 Vercel Sandbox 上为机构金融构建分析师 Agent。

https://claude.com/blog/claude-managed-agents-updates

---

## PODCASTS

**Unsupervised Learning: AI Vibe Check — Lab Wars, Why APIs Might Vanish & Future Predictions**

A wide-ranging roundtable with Jacob Efron, Ari (Datalogy, former DeepMind/Meta researcher), and Rob (Radical VC). Several key themes emerged:

**On open weights AI**: Rob argued that near-frontier open weights AI may be falling off — Meta is pulling back from open source, and Chinese labs (Qwen, DeepSeek) are now keeping their best models proprietary. Ari agreed but noted the bottleneck isn't capability but economics: "once you've achieved credibility, you want to start selling hosted inference, and opening up just undermines your business." Both expect fewer open models going forward.

**On the API business**: Ari made a provocative prediction — "it is not hard to imagine a world in which Anthropic is so compute constrained that they cut off the API." OpenAI is already selling futures for guaranteed inference access. The APIs might not disappear as a business decision but purely because of compute constraints. Rob agreed: labs may reserve their most powerful models for internal use only.

**On coding agents**: Ari noted engineers are shifting from individual contributors to "managers of agents" — context-switching between managing multiple agents rather than working on one thing. But the bottlenecks just shift: "it's a lot easier to produce massive amounts of code, but now you have this massive understanding gap."

**On the "apps are cooked" narrative**: Rob pushed back — "there's no way one or two or three companies will win every single important market." The labs will dominate horizontal cross-cutting areas (coding first), but vertical software for specific industries has staying power. Ari added that even frontier labs can't do everything — OpenAI suspending video efforts proves focus is necessary.

**On RSI (Recursive Self-Improvement)**: Ari has become more bullish, having seen promising results from agent-driven curation experiments. But he's skeptical of the runaway takeoff narrative — compute bottlenecks and the fact that 10+ companies have the talent and funding to work on it mean no single player will run away with it.

**On Anthropic's next big bet**: Rob predicted life sciences — "Anthropic will become one of the most important life sciences companies in the world." He noted rumors of Anthropic setting up wet lab facilities. Jacob countered that Isomorphic Labs (Demis Hassabis's company) already has a head start.

> **Unsupervised Learning：AI 氛围检查——实验室之争、API 可能消失与未来预测**
>
> Jacob Efron、Ari（Datalogy，前 DeepMind/Meta 研究员）和 Rob（Radical VC）之间的一场广泛圆桌讨论。几个关键主题浮出水面：
>
> **关于开源权重 AI**：Rob 认为接近前沿的开源权重 AI 可能正在衰落——Meta 正在从开源撤退，中国实验室（Qwen、DeepSeek）现在将其最佳模型保持为专有。Ari 同意但指出瓶颈不是能力而是经济性："一旦你获得了信誉，你就想开始销售托管推理服务，而开源只会破坏你的业务。"两人都预计未来的开源模型会越来越少。
>
> **关于 API 业务**：Ari 做出了一个 provocative 的预测——"不难想象一个 Anthropic 因算力受限而切断 API 的世界。"OpenAI 已经在出售未来推理访问的期货。API 可能不是作为商业决策消失，而纯粹是因为算力限制。Rob 同意：实验室可能只将最强大的模型保留供内部使用。
>
> **关于编码 Agent**：Ari 指出工程师正在从个人贡献者转变为"Agent 的管理者"——在管理多个 Agent 之间切换上下文，而不是专注于一件事。但瓶颈只是转移了："产生大量代码变得更容易了，但现在你有了巨大的理解差距。"
>
> **关于"应用已死"的叙事**：Rob 反驳道——"一两家或三家公司不可能赢得每一个重要市场。"实验室将主导横向跨领域（首先是编码），但面向特定行业的垂直软件仍有生命力。Ari 补充说，即使是前沿实验室也无法做所有事情——OpenAI 暂停视频项目证明了专注是必要的。
>
> **关于 RSI（递归自我改进）**：Ari 变得更加乐观，他从 Agent 驱动的数据策展实验中看到了有希望的结果。但他对失控式起飞叙事持怀疑态度——算力瓶颈以及 10 多家公司拥有人才和资金的事实意味着没有单一玩家能够一骑绝尘。
>
> **关于 Anthropic 的下一个大赌注**：Rob 预测是生命科学——"Anthropic 将成为世界上最重要的生命科学公司之一。"他提到有传言称 Anthropic 正在建立自己的湿实验室。Jacob 反驳说 Isomorphic Labs（Demis Hassabis 的公司）已经领先一步。

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W_iO8XxgD_I

# AI Builders Digest — June 22, 2026

## X / TWITTER

**OpenAI's Thibault Sottiaux** asked the community "What should we improve in the Codex app? What's not delightful?" — receiving 3,380 replies and 2,581 likes, an extraordinary engagement signal showing how deeply the developer community cares about the product. He also announced that Codex now supports banking unused usage resets, asking users whether they're hoarders or spenders (2,237 likes, 868 replies). The level of direct community engagement from a product leader at this scale is notable.

> OpenAI 的 Thibault Sottiaux 向社区提问"Codex 应用还需要改进什么？什么体验不够好？"——收到了 3,380 条回复和 2,581 个赞，这一非凡的互动信号显示了开发者社区对这个产品的深度关注。他还宣布 Codex 现在支持累积未使用的用量重置，询问用户是囤积者还是消耗者（2,237 个赞，868 条回复）。产品负责人以这种规模直接与社区互动，值得关注。

https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2068736857312198928
https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2068792010715324444

---

**Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch** observed that "coding agents will squeeze every ounce of IKEA effect out of you, if you let them" — the IKEA effect being the cognitive bias where people overvalue things they built themselves. The post received 1,335 likes. He also shared that the Vercel team optimized the ai-tools performance — "painting, layout, WebGPU shaders, blocking scripts. Every frame scrutinized" — with lessons to be published on the Vercel blog.

> Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch 观察到"如果你允许，编码 Agent 会榨干你每一丝宜家效应"——宜家效应是指人们高估自己亲手建造的东西的认知偏差。这条推文获得了 1,335 个赞。他还分享了 Vercel 团队优化了 ai-tools 的性能——"绘制、布局、WebGPU 着色器、阻塞脚本。每一帧都经过仔细检查"——优化经验将发布在 Vercel 博客上。

https://x.com/rauchg/status/2068778558672273422
https://x.com/rauchg/status/2068838709517336756

---

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** shared two sharp insights. First: "Agents will use software 100X more than people. When that happens, there's a huge need for guardrails... A simple query on any given agentic task could pull in more data than a user touches in a month." He argued that platforms that can move toward powering headless interactions will be best positioned. Second: he highlighted Sakana's new Fugu model — a mixture-of-models approach where a single API farms out work to the best-performing model for each task, handling model selection, delegation, verification, and synthesis automatically. "The complexity of a multi-agent system never reaches your code."

> Box CEO Aaron Levie 分享了两个犀利的观点。第一："Agent 使用软件的频率将比人类高出 100 倍。当这种情况发生时，对护栏的需求巨大……任何一个 Agent 任务的简单查询可能拉取的数据比用户一个月接触的还多。"他认为能够转向支持无头交互模式的平台将处于最佳位置。第二：他介绍了 Sakana 的新模型 Fugu——一种混合模型方法，通过单一 API 将工作分配给每个任务表现最好的模型，自动处理模型选择、委派、验证和合成。"多 Agent 系统的复杂性永远不会触及你的代码。"

https://x.com/levie/status/2068851573175021864
https://x.com/levie/status/2068917230570795178

---

**YC CEO Garry Tan** argued that "one underestimated thing... is how useful it is to have your own personal brain and company brain in 2026 at the dawn of usable AGI. AGI gives you the intelligence. You still have to collect your personal context to get the real unlock." He open-sourced GBrain to make this possible, receiving 964 likes.

> YC CEO Garry Tan 认为"一个被低估的事情是……在 2026 年可用 AGI 的黎明，拥有你自己的个人大脑和公司大脑是多么有用。AGI 给你智能。你仍然需要收集你的个人上下文才能获得真正的解锁。"他开源了 GBrain 来实现这一点，获得了 964 个赞。

https://x.com/garrytan/status/2068701356358308112

---

**Cursor Design's Ryo Lu** built a "Books" app in his ryOS platform — started in Cursor mobile, then hand-tuned animations and textures. The result: a digital bookshelf with wooden shelves that syncs progress with his ryOS account. A beautiful example of vibe-coding a polished, personal app.

> Cursor 设计师 Ryo Lu 在他的 ryOS 平台中构建了一个"Books"应用——从 Cursor 移动端开始，然后手动调整动画和纹理。结果是一个带有木质书架的数字书架，可与他的 ryOS 账户同步进度。这是一个用 vibe coding 打造精美个人应用的绝佳例子。

https://x.com/ryolu_/status/2068923971136098633

---

**Zara Zhang** shared a practical rule of thumb for preventing AI slop: "Is your input (the context) longer than the output? I've found that for the AI to produce quality results, my input is often 3-5 times the length of the output. If your input is much shorter than the output, it's almost certainly going to produce slop." She also clarified in a follow-up that she's talking about context, not prompt — a subtle but important distinction.

> Zara Zhang 分享了一个防止 AI 垃圾输出的实用经验法则："你的输入（上下文）比输出长吗？我发现要让 AI 产生高质量结果，我的输入通常是输出的 3-5 倍。如果你的输入远短于输出，几乎肯定会产出垃圾。"她还在后续推文中澄清，她说的是上下文而非提示词——一个微妙但重要的区别。

https://x.com/zarazhangrui/status/2068923768500793603
https://x.com/zarazhangrui/status/2068964055235321954

---

**OpenClaw's Peter Steinberger** addressed the narrative around OpenClaw: "The hype died down. We improved quality and grew a team. We created a non-profit whereas competitors are VC funded and have other agendas. This is our strongest week so far." He also expressed skepticism about multi-model routing, saying his "hunch was right" that it doesn't work as well as claimed.

> OpenClaw 的 Peter Steinberger 回应了关于 OpenClaw 的叙事："热度过去了。我们提高了质量，组建了团队。我们创建了一个非营利组织，而竞争对手是 VC 资助的，有其他议程。这是我们迄今为止最强的一周。"他还对多模型路由表示怀疑，称他的"直觉是对的"，即它并不像声称的那样有效。

https://x.com/steipete/status/2068961217524490739
https://x.com/steipete/status/2068960117253632160

---

**Peter Yang** shared an insight from Liu8in on why HTML turned out to be the foundation for agentic video making: "We learned the hard way that agents have no visual intelligence. So that's when we turned to code. HTML is the LLM's native language. LLMs can express not only information, but also visual aesthetics through HTML, CSS, and JavaScript."

> Peter Yang 分享了 Liu8in 关于为什么 HTML 成为 Agent 视频制作基础的观点："我们通过惨痛的经验学到，Agent 没有视觉智能。所以我们转向了代码。HTML 是 LLM 的母语。LLM 不仅能表达信息，还能通过 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript 表达视觉美学。"

https://x.com/petergyang/status/2068755908319236338

---

**Linear's Head of Product Nan Yu** shared a great framing: "Quality is irrational" — you have to have an irrational level of commitment to constantly choose quality, and an irrational level of self-belief that controlling things top to bottom yields better results than using common frameworks.

> Linear 产品负责人 Nan Yu 分享了一个精彩的框架："质量是非理性的"——你必须有一种非理性的承诺水平来持续选择质量，以及一种非理性的自信，认为从上到下控制一切比使用通用框架能产生更好的结果。

https://x.com/thenanyu/status/2068778750800531640

## PODCASTS

**Training Data: Google DeepMind's Logan Kilpatrick — Why the Model Eats the Harness**

Logan Kilpatrick, who runs Google AI Studio and the Gemini API, sat down for a wide-ranging conversation covering Google's agent strategy, coding models, world models, and the "model eats the harness" thesis.

**On Google's agent strategy**: Google is entering the "Gemini 3.5 era" where all products are being rebased to become "agentic native" — powered by the Antigravity agent harness. Antigravity is more than an IDE — it's an ecosystem (IDE, web agent-first experience, CLI, SDK) that also powers agentic features in Search, the Gemini app, and Google Cloud. Sundar framed this at IO as "the Agentic Gemini era."

**On the "model eats the harness" thesis**: Logan argued that what we call "the model" is no longer just weights — it's an expanding system around the weights (tool calling, search, code execution, agent harness). The scaffolding is often a few steps ahead of what's baked into the model, but "the model eats that scaffolding and it becomes part of the native model system." He predicted this will happen to the agent harness itself within 12 months — the alpha will shift elsewhere. He called for a "harness bench" to measure how well models adapt to different harnesses.

**On coding competition**: Logan acknowledged Google was behind on coding but argued the narrative changes fast — "December, the narrative was that Google had won" with Gemini 3. He pointed to the Windsurf acquisition and Antigravity as signs Google is taking coding seriously, and noted that Gemini 3.5 Flash — a post-training-only improvement — surpassed every previous pro model on coding. The "Avengers of AI" are working on it internally.

**On world models**: Omni is a single model (not routing to multiple models) that handles text, audio, video, and music. It's not real-time yet, but the first crank (Omni Flash) is already capable of stunning video editing — like seamlessly inserting a dog into a live stage talk. Logan emphasized Omni's philosophical appeal: "It's not changing me. It is changing a bunch of these other bits which are not me" — preserving authenticity while enhancing the environment.

**On the app layer**: Logan is bullish on startups. "Focus is the superpower of startups. If you can focus, you can do anything." He noted that 24 months ago people worried there'd be less opportunity for startups — "that feels like so far, in a way, not what has ended up playing out. If anything, it feels like there's even more opportunity."

> **Training Data：Google DeepMind 的 Logan Kilpatrick——为什么模型会吃掉编排层**
>
> 负责 Google AI Studio 和 Gemini API 的 Logan Kilpatrick 进行了一场广泛对话，涵盖了 Google 的 Agent 策略、编码模型、世界模型以及"模型吃掉编排层"的论点。
>
> **关于 Google 的 Agent 策略**：Google 正在进入"Gemini 3.5 时代"，所有产品都在重新构建为"Agent 原生"——由 Antigravity Agent 编排层驱动。Antigravity 不仅仅是一个 IDE——它是一个生态系统（IDE、Web Agent 优先体验、CLI、SDK），同时也为搜索、Gemini 应用和 Google Cloud 中的 Agent 功能提供支持。Sundar 在 IO 上将其称为"Agentic Gemini 时代"。
>
> **关于"模型吃掉编排层"论点**：Logan 认为我们所说的"模型"不再仅仅是权重——它是一个围绕权重的扩展系统（工具调用、搜索、代码执行、Agent 编排层）。脚手架通常比模型内置的内容领先几步，但"模型会吃掉那个脚手架，它成为原生模型系统的一部分"。他预测 Agent 编排层本身将在 12 个月内被模型消化——价值将转移到别处。他呼吁建立一个"编排层基准"来衡量模型适应不同编排层的能力。
>
> **关于编码竞争**：Logan 承认 Google 在编码方面落后了，但认为叙事变化很快——"去年 12 月，叙事是 Google 赢了"（Gemini 3 发布时）。他指出 Windsurf 收购和 Antigravity 是 Google 认真对待编码的信号，并提到 Gemini 3.5 Flash——仅通过后训练改进——在编码方面超越了之前的所有 Pro 模型。"AI 复仇者联盟"正在内部攻克这个问题。
>
> **关于世界模型**：Omni 是一个单一模型（不是路由到多个模型），处理文本、音频、视频和音乐。它还不是实时的，但第一个版本（Omni Flash）已经能够进行令人惊叹的视频编辑——比如无缝地将一只狗插入现场舞台演讲中。Logan 强调了 Omni 的哲学吸引力："它不是改变我。它改变的是那些不是我的其他部分"——在增强环境的同时保持真实性。
>
> **关于应用层**：Logan 对创业公司持乐观态度。"专注是创业公司的超能力。如果你能专注，你可以做任何事情。"他指出 24 个月前人们担心创业公司的机会会减少——"到目前为止，这似乎并没有发生。如果有的话，感觉机会甚至更多了。"

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cMAs8z2dehs

    75	          "url": "https://x.com/joshwoodward/status/2069408025362714957",

    82	        {
    83	          "id": "2069406832523624696",
    84	          "text": "Florida State University: \"Shortly after introducing NotebookLM on campus, we watched students who were struggling with a ‘C’ grade completely transform their study habits and their grades in a matter of weeks.\"\n\nhttps://t.co/xcPbvA37s0",
    85	          "createdAt": "2026-06-23T13:06:51.000Z",
    86	          "url": "https://x.com/joshwoodward/status/2069406832523624696",

    97	      "name": "Boris Cherny",

    99	      "bio": "Claude Code @anthropicai",
   100	      "tweets": [
   101	        {
   102	          "id": "2069474691010707486",
   103	          "text": "That's it. Point it at a channel, give it a task, and let it work. It's in beta on Slack today for Claude Enterprise and Team customers. More surfaces coming soon!\n\nLearn more: https://t.co/tcvNSCANlf",
   104	          "createdAt": "2026-06-23T17:36:29.000Z",
   105	          "url": "https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474691010707486",
   106	          "likes": 92,
   107	          "retweets": 4,
   108	          "replies": 17,
   109	          "isQuote": false,
   110	          "quotedTweetId": null
   111	        },
   112	        {
   113	          "id": "2069474689819480394",
   114	          "text": "Tag Claude in a channel, it spins up an instance with its own sandbox. It clones repos, writes code, tests, compiles all in that isolated environment and the sandbox gets thrown away when it's done. One instance per thread, its own memory and permissions per channel.",
   115	          "createdAt": "2026-06-23T17:36:29.000Z",
   116	          "url": "https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474689819480394",
   117	          "likes": 70,
   118	          "retweets": 1,
   119	          "replies": 5,
   120	          "isQuote": false,
   121	          "quotedTweetId": null
   122	        },
   123	        {
   124	          "id": "2069474688619958517",
   125	          "text": "I also have Claude monitoring Slack channels to proactively respond to people to answer their questions and draft PRs, and to react with specific emojis like ✅❌ when a thread is resolved.\n\nTo set it up, just add @.Claude to a channel and ask it to do this^, in natural language.",
   126	          "createdAt": "2026-06-23T17:36:29.000Z",
   127	          "url": "https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474688619958517",
   128	          "likes": 96,

   130	          "replies": 8,

   132	          "quotedTweetId": null
   133	        }
   134	      ]

Tool:      1	{
     2	  "generatedAt": "2026-06-24T07:29:10.617Z",

     4	  "podcasts": [
     5	    {
     6	      "source": "podcast",
     7	      "name": "No Priors",
     8	      "title": "Biohub: The Future of Biology is Open-Source with Co-Founders Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Head of Science Alex Rives",
     9	      "guid": "cfdceef6-642a-11f1-9b24-634dad16ced0",
    10	      "url": "https://www.youtube.com/@NoPriorsPodcast",
    11	      "publishedAt": "2026-06-10T13:00:00.000Z",
    12	      "transcript": "Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:02\nWe just wanna give tools to the whole scientific community.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 00:02 - 00:17\nWe wanna understand how biology works. I wanna understand the genetics of this person. I wanna understand the risks they have to different illnesses. My goal is to be able to treat the individual as an individual, understand the mechanisms, and be able to intervene.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 00:17 - 00:34\nWe'll have a bigger impact by getting us in more scientists hands quicker by doing it as open source projects instead. It's not just like there's some factory somewhere that you can pay to produce the data. You actually need to invent new novel scientific approaches. The theory isn't that we're gonna cure the diseases. We're not.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 00:34 - 00:36\nIt's that we wanna help accelerate the pace of progress for the\n\nSpeaker 3 | 00:36 - 00:42\nwhole scientific field. We folded over 1,100,000,000 proteins and predicted their structures,\n\nSpeaker 4 | 00:42 - 00:50\nand we didn't design a model for antibodies. We didn't design a model to be able to bind one particular target. We just designed a model that could understand proteins.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 00:50 - 00:58\nIf we could design a protein to actually change the physiology, then we can actually cure someone.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 01:05 - 01:18\nToday on No Priors, we're joined by Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Alex Reeves. We'll be talking about Biohub and all their various efforts to now start applying AI at scale to do world models of cells and different levels of interactions across biology.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 01:19 - 01:21\nMark, Priscilla, thank you for doing this.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 01:22 - 01:22\nThanks for having\n\nSpeaker 4 | 01:22 - 01:23\nIt us\n\nSpeaker 3 | 01:23 - 01:23\nwas fun.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 01:24 - 01:26\nCongratulations on new missions.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 01:26 - 01:26\nThank you.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 01:26 - 01:41\nYou guys made Biohub your primary philanthropic effort and then committed $500,000,000 to this virtual biology initiative. Can you tell us a little bit about, you know, why do that and how did you go from we should fund this to this is like\n\nSpeaker 2 | 01:41 - 02:04\nwho we are? So Biohub in its current form, we're super excited about. We feel like it's a really good fit for who we are and what we bring to the table and what we can achieve together. But this work started ten years ago when we were thinking about how can we give back? And wanted to build an organization that could cure, prevent, and manage all disease by the end of the century.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 02:05 - 02:15\nAnd we had a series of hilarious meetings with scientists that, like, famous Nobel Prize winning scientists were just laughing at us.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 02:15 - 02:17\nWas that your starting line? We're just gonna cure all disease?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:17 - 02:34\nNo. No. And to be clear, we don't think that we're gonna be the ones curing the diseases. Our goal is always to build tools that could accelerate the whole scientific field, so that way the scientific field collectively could cure all the diseases. But still people thought that by the end of the century it was a stretch.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:34 - 02:35\nNow I think it's like too conservative.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 02:36 - 02:56\nAnd so we kept being like, okay, well, we had these series of funny, awkward educational conversations where we were like, okay, but like, why? Like, why do you think it's impossible? And like, you know, just being the person in the room is just like, oh, I don't know why. You tell me. Finally, we got people to like, they're like, fine.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 02:56 - 03:08\nIf you really must know. And we're like, you know, we do. It seems important. You know, they were like, well, we work in silos. And when you publish, information doesn't get shared.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 03:08 - 03:32\nIt gets locked up for long periods of time. And we don't have tooling. You know, they gave the example of like, we build a great tool by one postdoc in a lab and it lives on their computer. And when they graduate, the tool is gone. And we heard was very hard to build shared tools to move science faster, build a shared knowledge base to quickly move science faster.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 03:33 - 03:39\nAnd that's sort of where we began in thinking about, okay, like if those are the problems, like what can we contribute?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:40 - 04:03\nYeah. I mean, so the original Biohub model was basically focus on long term tool development by bringing together engineers and scientists across multiple universities to focus on long term tool development. And basically, it like worked. And, you know, we started off with with CZI doing a number of different things. And I think over time, we just felt like, okay.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:03 - 04:42\nThe science piece is really working, and we just kept on investing more and more and more in it until now it is basically the primary and main thing that we're doing. And we'v
  12.1	e expanded the original San Francisco Biohub to a handful now at this point. There's New York. There's Chicago. The real focus and the unifying theme at this point the virtual biology initiative around taking the unique data sets that are able to be generated in order to model effectively starting with the smallest pieces of proteins, but then eventually cells and whole biological systems.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:42 - 05:24\nBut that's kind of how we've evolved, this idea that we talk about around that some of this is an AI problem, and you want to build a frontier AI lab, but you need to couple that with a frontier biology effort that can do the work of basically being able to understand and get the data that you need to actually be able to build these models. Because unlike language models, there's just like a lot of data out there on the Internet. That's not really the case with biology. I mean, there are obviously a bunch of different data sets that exist that academia and scientists have generated over the the decades, but a lot of the stuff that I think we wanna put into this, it doesn't exist. Right?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 05:24 - 06:10\nIt's like you wanna be able to visualize things that people haven't been able to see before, which is why we were doing the the imaging work. You wanna be able to record things that are going on inside the body, which is why we're doing the kind of cellular engineering work. Or you want to be able to measure things like inflammation in ways that haven't been possible, which is why the Chicago Biohub is focused on building those kind of devices and being able to do that. And that will fundamentally create new types of datasets that will allow new types of models. And I think it's just a very exciting thing that, going back to what you're saying, if the scientific field, it primarily needs kind of tool development that now is going to empower scientists across the field to be able to do their work faster, that's what we think we can provide through this kind of long term focus on tool development.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 06:10 - 07:08\nBut I think there's a fun through line on where we started and, you know, bringing us to our work that Alex is driving now, is that our very first request for application, RFA here, was around single cell sequencing. And we wanted to look at sort of like the RNA that is transcribed in individual cells. And that was possible, but it was still pretty early on in understanding how different cells were expressing their DNA to the point where at the beginning we were just funding methods, like getting people to describe how to do it so that others could share that methodology. And then that became us funding the Human Cell Atlas, which is now one of the largest databases of single cell transcriptomes, it was getting hard for scientists to annotate the data. So we built CellByGene, which was like a very simple annotation tool that scientists could use to make use of that data.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 07:08 - 07:47\nThen a community came around Cell by Gene, built around Cell by Gene, and started contributing more and more data that we had nothing to do with sort of creating or funding or making happen in the world. And now Cell by Gene is a corpus of knowledge that a lot of the transcriptomic based models are based off of and is used regularly by the scientific community. But still there are always critiques. Like, this is just stamp collecting. Like, you're just gathering bits of knowledge sorry, bits of data, and we're not going to be able to pull scientific knowledge and wisdom and insights out of.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 07:47 - 08:25\nAnd we're like, well, we didn't have an answer for a while. And then imagine our delight when large language models became a huge topic of conversation that could make sense of large amounts of data. And I just for me, it was like, what if we could actually understand how biology worked? Move it from a discovery based science to an engineering based science where we could systematically understand how living beings, living cells worked and be able to understand why things go wrong. And so when we saw that moment, we're like, this is it.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 08:25 - 08:27\nSomething really big could happen here.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 08:27 - 08:43\nAlex, you started at MetaFair, but you were on the path to, you know, you'd assemble the team at evolutionary scale and you'd raise venture and you were making progress in your models. What was the pitch from Mark and Priscilla where you said like, that's actually the right way to go after the mission?\n\nSpeaker 3 | 08:43 - 09:30\nWell, I think for me it was really kind of the moment when I understood that, you know, they they really saw this as as an integration of frontier AI and frontier biology. And I think I had developed conviction that, you know, this is really a new era of science that's just beginning, kind of what's gonna be possible with artificial intelligence. And, you know, we're in the age of informati
  12.2	on theory at scale, and we have these systems that can basically kind of predict the next token, and they can, learn world models from that. They can learn biology from the data. And so, you know, I think that it was really clear that, you know, to build kind of that next kind of institution for the next era, you would really need to have frontier artificial intelligence.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 09:30 - 09:45\nYou would have to have frontier biology. You would need to start to put those things in feedback and really have models that are learning from the biology. And I think, you know, And you need the right scale on the right people. And so this just really felt, I think, like the way to do that.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 09:45 - 10:09\nThere's a variety of different models that you all have been working on. And I think it's kind of interesting because some of the earliest breakthroughs in biology were things like AlphaFold, where there was a Google model that showed that you could do protein folding at scale in a really interesting way that people didn't realize was very tractable. And this was pre sort of the really big transformer waves that came later. And then you're working on a variety of different things at different scale, right? You're doing incremental molecular modeling and protein folding.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 10:09 - 10:25\nYou're doing cell based stuff. You're thinking about interrogating larger scale systems in biology. How well do you think that extends from sort of the micro to the macro? You mentioned almost starting with building blocks and building up, but modeling cellular behavior is very different from modeling protein folding. The data is very different, the modeling is different.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 10:25 - 10:35\nI'm just curious, do you think it's all similar in terms of it's just data and you train stuff? Or do you think there's some differences in terms of how you actually have to deal with these systems?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 10:35 - 10:50\nI mean, are probably some differences. I mean, you can probably talk more to the specifics around this. But I mean, I think each layer is gonna end up being somewhat qualitatively different. Right? But you need to be able to understand the protein interactions in order to be able to understand how cells work.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 10:50 - 11:20\nSo you can't just go straight to cells in a way without understanding the protein modeling. And then if you're trying to understand something like the, you know, the way the immune system works or a bunch of cells interact together, then, you know, it's tough to do that without first understanding cells. I mean, you might be able to, at, like, a very high level of abstraction, simulate a system. But if you really wanna, like, understand how it's gonna work, you kind of wanna build the simulations at each level hierarchically. So that's basically the approach that we're going through starting with the building blocks and the and the protein.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 11:20 - 11:34\nBut yeah. I mean, I think that there's gonna be different types of data that you wanna collect for each. The modeling techniques, think we'll see. I mean, that'll all keep on advancing across the board. But I do think that, like, a big part of the strategy is this view that you need to build it up hierarchically.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 11:35 - 12:13\nAnd, you know, one of the things that's unique about us in this space is we are very intentional that the AI efforts and the wet lab efforts were a single effort. And we've done a lot of work to bring them together. And the really neat thing that we can do is really try to pull and gather data that helps us connect across sort of the hierarchy. You know, you can look at transcriptomics with space within a cell and look at where it's localizing. We can look at translucent zebrafish and look at the development across different cells and when the brain develops.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 12:13 - 12:35\nWe have sensors that allow us to look at cell cell communication in different molecules. And so we can be strategic about the types of experiments and data we want to collect that helps us bridge across these, that makes it so that there's some connective tissue that helps drive the modeling that you know, the modeling magic that happens.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 12:35 - 12:40\nYeah. The reason I asked the question, by the way, is I used to be a biologist. I have a PhD in biology, and I worked in wet labs for almost a decade and everything else.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 12:40 - 12:42\nAre looking for a job.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 12:46 - 12:48\nWe can talk about that later.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 12:48 - 12:49\nIt's not a no. At\n\nSpeaker 5 | 12:49 - 12:49\nthis point\n\nSpeaker 1 | 12:49 - 12:51\nin my career, know?\n\nSpeaker 2 | 12:51 - 12:52\nHearing, I\n\nSpeaker 1 | 12:52 - 12:53\nlove my aggressive reaction.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 12:53 - 13:15\nI'm like Danny Glover, you know, and Lisa Waffen. I'm almost at retirement. But I think one of the things that was always lacking was this integra
  12.3	tive nature across the different layers of biology, and the developmental biologists would work on their own, the molecular biologists would be doing different experiments. And so that's what I was curious about. Typically, there's a reductionist view of biology and there's a systems view, and those people didn't really work together deeply.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 13:15 - 13:20\nAnd so one of the exciting things about what you're doing actually is how you're bridging that. And so that was kind of the basis for the question as well.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 13:20 - 14:14\nYeah. And if I could add something there, you know, it's I think that, you know, we're in the age of this kind of information theory in biology. And so, you know, there there are levels of of complexity and hierarchy in biology, and kind of each level is is made up of and, you know, constituted by the lower levels. And so as you want to have that kind of more complete description and you want to have systems that can really generalize and begin to actually answer, you know, experimental questions digitally that you could ask in the lab, you need to have kind of the right basis for modeling at every level. And so I think what's really unique about what we can do is to, as Priscilla and Mark were saying, really build information at each of these different layers, collect them, collect kind of those connection points, but then also really kind of do it at the scale that will reveal that underlying information architecture.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 14:14 - 14:21\nAnd that's gonna be really critical to actually be able to build digital representations that can answer new experimental questions.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 14:22 - 15:01\nOne of the things that inspires me most about this effort is really what Priscilla said, which is like, well, there's so much we actually understand about biology and what if we could? Which I think is actually very different from lots of other incredibly interesting and useful AI problems we attack where we're like trying to replicate human behavior. I'm like, a lot of that data's, you know, on the internet or captured. And without pretending to understand all human behavior, can predict a lot of it. I thought one of the most interesting things in your release was actually, you know, the mechanistic interpretability stuff you alluded to, which is, can we actually extract new knowledge from, you know, what the model believes is happening?\n\nSpeaker 4 | 15:01 - 15:03\nRight? Can you talk a little bit about that?\n\nSpeaker 3 | 15:03 - 15:34\nYeah, I'm really excited about that. So I think, you know, in mechanistic interpretability, kind of traditionally it's been applied to large language models with the goal of understanding, you know, kind of what is the representation space of a large language model? How does it compute things, and does that really connect to, you know, what we understand about our intuitive understanding of the world? And so there's, I think, this really rich toolkit that has been developed to start to be able to ask those questions. So kind of what does that mean for biology?\n\nSpeaker 3 | 15:34 - 16:05\nOne of the classes of models that we train are these protein language models. So they're really, you know, trained on the codes of proteins. And so anything they learn about biology is is kind of emergent. And we've seen that they can learn things like biological structure and biological function, and that's just kind of emergent from this, you know, token prediction training task. So, you know, as we think about, like, mechanistic interpretability in those models, you know, we're really seeing the unknown because the models have been trained on billions of protein sequences.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 16:05 - 16:36\nThey've been trained on, you know, both known and unknown biology. And yet they're developing these representations that start to kind of capture things that we can really see correspond to that reductive picture of biology that's been built up over the centuries. So kind of you can start to connect the dots between proteins where we kind of really don't know anything about them with proteins where we do know something because there's that kind of underlying structure grammar that's linking them in the representation space of the model.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 16:37 - 16:47\nAnd at the extreme, it could be, you know, we're going to understand systems in the body that we didn't before or the mechanism of action for a new treatment because we can ask the model, right, interrogate that representation.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 16:47 - 16:58\nThat's right. The hope is that you kind of really learn the underlying basis for how it's making the predictions. And so you open up the black box and you can actually understand kind of the biology that the model is representing.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 16:58 - 17:23\nSo asking for a friend, you know, you guys all believe in venture backed companies as a way to have impact on the world. Was it like collecting data on zebra
  12.4	fish or the span of the data, or the wet lab work, or just the scale? Like what makes this a better fit for this big nonprofit, you know, ecosystem effort versus a venture backed company?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 17:24 - 17:27\nWell, I think we just want to give tools to the whole scientific community.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 17:27 - 17:29\nAnd I mean, like, so I I think\n\nSpeaker 1 | 17:29 - 17:54\nin order to have the biggest impact, I mean, part of it is just we're I mean, it's not actually clear that we couldn't run it as a business if we wanted to. I just think that we'll have a bigger impact by getting this in more scientists' hands quicker by doing it as open source projects instead. So, yeah, I mean, I think that that's kind of the approach. But I don't know. It's an interesting question.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 17:54 - 18:28\nI'm not sure that I mean, obviously, you were doing it as a for profit company, a bunch of the modeling before, then you run into certain issues. You have to raise a large amount of money in order to build the compute clusters. I think in a lot of ways, the data is actually even more of a constraint.    406	          "text": "Good post on the dynamics of AI pricing right now. We’re basically going to see a barbell dynamic on pricing between high cost frontier models and cheap but good open or closed weights models. \n\nThen the question is how do you maximize efficiency from the frontier models, and get better quality and performance of the cheaper models. That’s where the applied AI layer comes in.\n\nThe applied layer can route to the best model at any time given the workload at hand, as well as mitigate either high token costs or worse performance. The closer you are to the underlying workflow, the better you can tune the use of models for a given business process. \n\nThat’s all about understanding the workflows, domain specific FDEs, evals by customer or process, getting data setup well for agents, and more. AI: Now I have all the data. Let me compose the bilingual digest for June 24, 2026.

---

# AI Builders Digest — June 24, 2026

## X / TWITTER

**Andrej Karpathy** declared that Claude Tag represents the "3rd major redesign of LLM UI/UX." The first paradigm was the LLM as a website you visit; the second was the LLM as an app you download; the third is "a self-contained, persistent, asynchronous entity with org-wide tools and context, working alongside teams of humans." He described how once you do the engineering work to make it "just work" across tools, integrations, compute environments, memory, and security, "Claude basically joins the team in a seamless way — you can talk to it as you would talk to a person." The post received 12,492 likes — by far the most-engaged tweet in this batch.

> Andrej Karpathy 宣布 Claude Tag 代表了"LLM UI/UX 的第三次重大重新设计。"第一个范式是 LLM 作为你要访问的网站；第二个范式是 LLM 作为你要下载的应用；第三个范式是"一个自包含、持久化、异步的实体，拥有组织级工具和上下文，与人类团队并肩工作。"他描述了当你完成让它在工具、集成、计算环境、内存和安全性之间"正常工作"的工程工作后，"Claude 基本上以无缝的方式加入了团队——你可以像与人交谈一样与它对话。"这条推文获得了 12,492 个赞——是本批中互动量最高的推文。

https://x.com/karpathy/status/2069547676849557725

---

**Anthropic's Boris Cherny** announced Claude Tag for Slack — tag @Claude in a channel, and it spins up an instance with its own sandbox, clones repos, writes code, tests, and compiles in an isolated environment. Each thread gets its own instance with its own memory and permissions. He also demonstrated having Claude proactively monitor Slack channels to answer questions, draft PRs, and react with emojis (✅❌) when threads are resolved. It's in beta for Claude Enterprise and Team customers today.

> Anthropic 的 Boris Cherny 宣布了 Claude Tag for Slack——在频道中 @Claude，它会启动一个带有自己沙箱的实例，克隆仓库、编写代码、测试和编译，全部在隔离环境中完成。每个线程都有自己的实例、内存和权限。他还演示了让 Claude 主动监控 Slack 频道来回答问题、起草 PR，并在线程解决时用表情符号（✅❌）做出反应。该功能今天面向 Claude Enterprise 和 Team 客户开放测试。

https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474691010707486
https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474689819480394
https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474688619958517

---

**Anthropic's Cat Wu** shared that Claude Tag's internal version at Anthropic merges 65% of product PRs, calling it "a paradigm shift in how we ship products." She emphasized it's "our first product that is natively multi-player and proactive," and shared a guide for configuring agent permissions.

> Anthropic 的 Cat Wu 分享说，Claude Tag 在 Anthropic 内部的版本合并了 65% 的产品 PR，称其为"我们交付产品方式的范式转变。"她强调这是"我们第一个原生支持多人协作和主动性的产品，"并分享了配置 Agent 权限的指南。

https://x.com/_catwu/status/2069473118742331608
https://x.com/_catwu/status/2069484330938998993

---

**Anthropic's Alex Albert** echoed Karpathy's sentiment: "This has completely changed how I work with Claude. It feels less like using a tool and more like managing a team."

> Anthropic 的 Alex Albert 呼应了 Karpathy 的感受："这完全改变了我与 Claude 的合作方式。感觉不像在使用工具，而更像在管理一个团队。"

https://x.com/alexalbert__/status/2069470389391241314

---

**Anthropic's Thariq** shared practical tips for using Claude Tag — setting up a "scheduling" channel where Claude finds time on calendars, using pinned messages for status tracking, and having Claude react with emojis (⏲️✅❓🛑) so you can see its status at a glance.

> Anthropic 的 Thariq 分享了使用 Claude Tag 的实用技巧——设置一个"日程安排"频道让 Claude 在日历上找时间，使用置顶消息进行状态跟踪，以及让 Claude 用表情符号（⏲️✅❓🛑）做出反应，让你一目了然地看到它的状态。

https://x.com/trq212/status/2069474343512617390
https://x.com/trq212/status/2069474339679052144

---

**OpenAI's Thibault Sottiaux** shared that Codex "had a bug. Fixed." — receiving 3,249 likes and 279 replies, showing the community's appetite for rapid iteration. He also joked about the intensity of working at OpenAI: "If you are at OpenAI, you would know that this never happens. Be right back celebrating 5 minutes of peace."

> OpenAI 的 Thibault Sottiaux 分享说 Codex "之前有个 bug。已修复。"——获得了 3,249 个赞和 279 条回复，显示了社区对快速迭代的渴望。他还调侃了在 OpenAI 工作的强度："如果你在 OpenAI，你会知道这种事永远不会发生。我先去庆祝这 5 分钟的平静。"

https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2069579993588625574
https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2069624530960838914

---

**Peter Yang** asked a provocative question: "What is design if what's accessing your product is just an agent looking for an API or CLI?" He also discovered the Google Workspace CLI and called it "super useful," wondering why it isn't celebrated more. And he observed that "human-agent interaction is basically trending towards managing a highly capable employee" — "1 on 1s and performance reviews for Claude next?"

> Peter Yang 提出了一个 provocative 的问题："如果访问你产品的只是一个寻找 API 或 CLI 的 Agent，那设计是什么？"他还发现了 Google Workspace CLI，称其"超级有用"，想知道为什么它没有得到更多关注。他还观察到"人机交互基本上正在朝着管理一名高能力员工的方向发展"——"接下来要给 Claude 做一对一和绩效评估了吗？"

https://x.com/petergyang/status/2069603490524254473
https://x.com/petergyang/status/2069551302246592799
https://x.com/petergyang/status/2069530765352907180

---

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** analyzed the barbell dynamic in AI pricing: high-cost frontier models on one end, cheap open/closed weights models on the other. The applied layer's job is to route between them — maximizing efficiency from frontier models while improving quality from cheaper ones. "The closer you are to the underlying workflow, the better you can tune the use of models for a given business process." He also highlighted Claude Tag's integration with Box — making enterprise content a "portable knowledge base" accessible from Slack.

> Box CEO Aaron Levie 分析了 AI 定价中的杠铃动态：一端是高成本前沿模型，另一端是便宜的开源/闭源权重模型。应用层的工作是在两者之间路由——最大化前沿模型的效率，同时提高廉价模型的质量。"你越接近底层工作流，就越能针对特定业务流程优化模型的使用。"他还强调了 Claude Tag 与 Box 的集成——使企业内容成为可从 Slack 访问的"便携知识库"。

https://x.com/levie/status/2069639600310767616
https://x.com/levie/status/2069596515560267891

---

**YC CEO Garry Tan** called Linzumi "Codex but actually multiplayer — magical for teams," noting its founder Sean Grove was on the OpenAI team that greatly reduced sycophancy in ChatGPT. He also called out Dropbox for not supporting larger than 3TB plans — "the amount of data we are throwing off that is actually usable is going to go exponential from AI."

> YC CEO Garry Tan 称 Linzumi 是"Codex 但真正支持多人协作——对团队来说很神奇，"并指出其创始人 Sean Grove 曾在 OpenAI 团队大幅减少了 ChatGPT 中的讨好行为。他还指出 Dropbox 不支持超过 3TB 的方案——"我们产生的实际可用的数据量将因 AI 而呈指数级增长。"

https://x.com/garrytan/status/2069474420113146355
https://x.com/garrytan/status/2069434452628185241

---

**Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch** announced a private X Chat group for eve feedback, inviting builders with "high quality critical feedback or requirements" to DM him for direct access to eve engineers. He also shared that Vercel and Cursor are partnering.

> Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch 宣布为 eve 反馈建立一个私密 X 聊天群组，邀请"有高质量关键反馈或需求"的构建者私信他，以获得与 eve 工程师的直接沟通渠道。他还分享了 Vercel 和 Cursor 正在合作的消息。

https://x.com/rauchg/status/2069590431646769472
https://x.com/rauchg/status/2069513849578082474

**Replit CEO Amjad Masad** celebrated a team achievement with a reflective note: "It starts with a prompt — it's a whole lot of work to get here — but it starts with an idea."

> Replit CEO Amjad Masad 庆祝了一项团队成就，并附上反思："从一个提示开始——要走到这里需要大量工作——但它始于一个想法。"

https://x.com/amasad/status/2069588152285794373

**Swyx** noted that Zai (Zhipu AI) IPO'd in January at HK$120/share, and their GLM model has now "beat DeepSeek with the world's undisputed top open model and in some respects say top model period." He shared excitement about their team returning to San Francisco for aiDotEngineer.

> Swyx 指出智谱 AI（Zai）于今年 1 月以每股 120 港元上市，其 GLM 模型现已"击败了 DeepSeek，成为世界上无可争议的最佳开源模型，在某些方面甚至可以说是最佳模型。"他对他们的团队将重返旧金山参加 aiDotEngineer 表示兴奋。

https://x.com/swyx/status/2069598378191941835

**Google VP Josh Woodward** shared that Florida State University reported students who were struggling with a "C" grade completely transformed their study habits and grades within weeks of using NotebookLM on campus.

> Google VP Josh Woodward 分享说，佛罗里达州立大学报告称，在校园中使用 NotebookLM 后，原本成绩挣扎在"C"级的学生在几周内完全改变了他们的学习习惯和成绩。

## PODCASTS

**No Priors: Biohub — The Future of Biology is Open-Source with Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Alex Rives**

Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Biohub's Head of Science Alex Rives joined No Priors for a deep conversation on building open-source AI for biology at massive scale.

**The origin story**: Chan and Zuckerberg started Biohub ten years ago with the goal to "cure, prevent, and manage all disease by the end of the century." Nobel Prize-winning scientists laughed at them. "Now I think it's too conservative," Zuckerberg said. The early focus was on tool-building — single-cell sequencing, the Human Cell Atlas, and CellByGene — which grew into a corpus used by the entire scientific community.

**The strategy: frontier AI + frontier biology**: Zuckerberg described their approach as building hierarchical world models of biology — starting with proteins, then cells, then whole biological systems. Unlike language models where data is abundant on the internet, biology data must be invented: "You need to invent new novel scientific approaches to be able to observe the things that create the data that go into the model." This is why Biohub combines an AI research team with wet labs doing cellular engineering (New York), imaging (San Francisco), and inflammation measurement devices (Chicago).

**ESM Fold: a world model for proteins**: Alex Rives's team released ESM Fold — a protein language model trained on billions of sequences that learns emergent representations of protein biology. It folded over 1.1 billion proteins and predicted their structures at atomic resolution. The model is blazing fast and hits state-of-the-art on structure prediction, protein-protein interactions, and antibody design. Most striking: they didn't design a model for antibodies — they "just designed a model that could understand proteins, and you get protein design as an emergent property." They validated their designs in the lab with cryo-EM, finding nanomolar binders.

**Mechanistic interpretability in biology**: Rives is applying techniques from LLM interpretability to protein models — opening the black box to understand what biology the model has learned. Because the model trains on billions of sequences spanning known and unknown biology, it develops representations that connect proteins we know nothing about to proteins we understand, revealing "underlying structure grammar."

**Why open source**: Zuckerberg was clear: "We just want to give tools to the whole scientific community." Running it as a nonprofit avoids the constraints of venture funding — the 10-15 year time horizon, the need to invent new science to generate data, and the desire to put tools in the hands of everyone, including researchers working on rare diseases. "Our vision is not that there's going to be some central superintelligence that solves all of science."

**The ultimate goal**: Priscilla Chan framed it as treating the individual as an individual — understanding the full chain from gene variant to protein to disease process, then designing a bespoke intervention. "If we could design a protein to actually change the physiology, then we can actually cure someone." The next big challenge: the virtual cell — modeling each level of biological complexity (proteomic, genetic, transcriptomic) and connecting it to phenotype.

> **No Priors：Biohub——生物学的未来是开源的，与 Mark Zuckerberg、Priscilla Chan 和 Alex Rives 的对话**
>
> Mark Zuckerberg、Priscilla Chan 和 Biohub 科学负责人 Alex Rives 在 No Priors 上进行了一场深度对话，探讨如何大规模构建用于生物学的开源 AI。
>
> **起源故事**：Chan 和 Zuckerberg 十年前创立了 Biohub，目标是"在本世纪末治愈、预防和管理所有疾病。"诺贝尔奖得主科学家们嘲笑他们。Zuckerberg 说："现在我认为这个目标太保守了。"早期的重点是工具建设——单细胞测序、人类细胞图谱和 CellByGene——这些工具后来发展成为整个科学界使用的知识库。
>
> **策略：前沿 AI + 前沿生物学**：Zuckerberg 将他们的方法描述为构建生物学的分层世界模型——从蛋白质开始，然后是细胞，再到整个生物系统。与互联网上数据丰富的语言模型不同，生物学数据必须被创造："你需要发明新的、新颖的科学方法，才能观察到那些构成模型数据的东西。"这就是为什么 Biohub 将 AI 研究团队与进行细胞工程（纽约）、成像（旧金山）和炎症测量设备（芝加哥）的湿实验室结合起来。
>
> **ESM Fold：蛋白质的世界模型**：Alex Rives 的团队发布了 ESM Fold——一个在数十亿条序列上训练的蛋白质语言模型，能够学习蛋白质生物学的涌现表示。它折叠了超过 11 亿个蛋白质，并以原子分辨率预测了它们的结构。该模型速度极快，在结构预测、蛋白质-蛋白质相互作用和抗体设计方面达到了最先进水平。最引人注目的是：他们没有专门设计抗体模型——他们"只是设计了一个能够理解蛋白质的模型，而蛋白质设计作为涌现属性自然产生。"他们在实验室用冷冻电镜验证了设计，发现了纳摩尔级别的结合物。
>
> **生物学中的机制可解释性**：Rives 正在将 LLM 可解释性的技术应用于蛋白质模型——打开黑箱，理解模型学到了什么生物学知识。由于模型在跨越已知和未知生物学的数十亿条序列上训练，它发展出了将我们一无所知的蛋白质与已知蛋白质联系起来的表示，揭示了"底层的结构语法"。
>
> **为什么开源**：Zuckerberg 非常明确："我们只是想给整个科学界提供工具。"以非营利方式运营避免了风险投资的约束——10-15 年的时间跨度、需要发明新科学来生成数据，以及希望将工具交到每个人手中，包括研究罕见疾病的研究人员。"我们的愿景不是会有某个中央超级智能来解决所有科学问题。"
>
> **最终目标**：Priscilla Chan 将其表述为将每个个体作为个体来对待——理解从基因变异到蛋白质再到疾病过程的完整链条，然后设计定制化的干预方案。"如果我们能够设计一种蛋白质来实际改变生理机能，那么我们就能真正治愈一个人。"下一个重大挑战：虚拟细胞——对生物复杂性的每个层面（蛋白质组、基因组、转录组）进行建模，并将其与表型联系起来。

https://www.youtube.com/@NoPriorsPodcast

---

**Note**: The podcast URL in the feed points to the channel page rather than a specific video. The episode title is "Biohub: The Future of Biology is Open-Source with Co-Founders Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Head of Science Alex Rives."

    84	          "text": "Remote / local handoff in Codex! Removing boundaries one at a time. \n\nWhen you let the model be in the driver seat, you actually need less infrastructure. https://t.co/sfFYixfXPl",

   100	      "tweets": [
   101	        {
   102	          "id": "2068175172960690266",
   103	          "text": "I used to be a die-hard Claude Code user.\n\nCodex has won me over because:\n\n→ GPT-5.5 is excellent\n→ Fast mode + generous limits = more reps\n→ Little touches like steering, auto remote control on phone, etc\n\nBut most of all Codex's browser and computer use capabilities are simply goated. I built so many workflows relying on those two things alone instead of hunting for APIs.\n\nI still use Claude Code too. The app seems to be getting better and the design and frontend capability of Opus is still much better than GPT. Whenever Fable comes back that's another reason to go back.\n\nHonestly, I hope these two compete forever and other players (Cursor/Grok, Gemini, etc) all stay competitive.\n\nThis way the builder keeps winning 🙂",
   104	          "createdAt": "2026-06-20T03:32:40.000Z",

   129	      "bio": "Philosopher & ethicist trying to make AI be good @AnthropicAI.\nPersonal account. All opinions come from my training data.",
   130	      "tweets": [
   131	        {
   132	          "id": "2068218515723866477",
   133	          "text": "I had chronic pain for most of my life until a doctor did an MRI of the pain source and found a congenital condition that was then fixed with surgery. Now I'm wondering if I had 30+ years of pain because doctors worried I was too stupid to be in the presence of scan results.",
   134	          "createdAt": "2026-06-20T06:24:54.000Z",
   135	          "url": "https://x.com/AmandaAskell/status/2068218515723866477",
   136	          "likes": 561,
   137	          "retweets": 19,
   138	          "replies": 28,
   139	          "isQuote": false,
   140	          "quotedTweetId": null
   141	        },
   142	        {
   143	          "id": "2068162192927756544",
   144	          "text": "A counter to this is \"yes but people *don't* ignore it\". But not ignoring things on scans is our norm because, until recently, we only did scans if there was a clear need.
