     4	  "podcasts": [
     5	    {
     6	      "source": "podcast",
     7	      "name": "No Priors",
     8	      "title": "Biohub: The Future of Biology is Open-Source with Co-Founders Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Head of Science Alex Rives",
     9	      "guid": "cfdceef6-642a-11f1-9b24-634dad16ced0",
    10	      "url": "https://www.youtube.com/@NoPriorsPodcast",
    11	      "publishedAt": "2026-06-10T13:00:00.000Z",
    12	      "transcript": "Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:02\nWe just wanna give tools to the whole scientific community.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 00:02 - 00:17\nWe wanna understand how biology works. I wanna understand the genetics of this person. I wanna understand the risks they have to different illnesses. My goal is to be able to treat the individual as an individual, understand the mechanisms, and be able to intervene.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 00:17 - 00:34\nWe'll have a bigger impact by getting us in more scientists hands quicker by doing it as open source projects instead. It's not just like there's some factory somewhere that you can pay to produce the data. You actually need to invent new novel scientific approaches. The theory isn't that we're gonna cure the diseases. We're not.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 00:34 - 00:36\nIt's that we wanna help accelerate the pace of progress for the\n\nSpeaker 3 | 00:36 - 00:42\nwhole scientific field. We folded over 1,100,000,000 proteins and predicted their structures,\n\nSpeaker 4 | 00:42 - 00:50\nand we didn't design a model for antibodies. We didn't design a model to be able to bind one particular target. We just designed a model that could understand proteins.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 00:50 - 00:58\nIf we could design a protein to actually change the physiology, then we can actually cure someone.\n\nSpeaker 5 | 01:05 - 01:18\nToday on No Priors, we're joined by Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Alex Reeves. We'll be talking about Biohub and all their various efforts to now start applying AI at scale to do world models of cells and different levels of interactions across biology.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 01:19 - 01:21\nMark, Priscilla, thank you for doing this.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 01:22 - 01:22\nThanks for having\n\nSpeaker 4 | 01:22 - 01:23\nIt us\n\nSpeaker 3 | 01:23 - 01:23\nwas fun.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 01:24 - 01:26\nCongratulations on new missions.\n\nSpeaker 3 | 01:26 - 01:26\nThank you.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 01:26 - 01:41\nYou guys made Biohub your primary philanthropic effort and then committed $500,000,000 to this virtual biology initiative. Can you tell us a little bit about, you know, why do that and how did you go from we should fund this to this is like\n\nSpeaker 2 | 01:41 - 02:04\nwho we are? So Biohub in its current form, we're super excited about. We feel like it's a really good fit for who we are and what we bring to the table and what we can achieve together. But this work started ten years ago when we were thinking about how can we give back? And wanted to build an organization that could cure, prevent, and manage all disease by the end of the century.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 02:05 - 02:15\nAnd we had a series of hilarious meetings with scientists that, like, famous Nobel Prize winning scientists were just laughing at us.\n\nSpeaker 4 | 02:15 - 02:17\nWas that your starting line? We're just gonna cure all disease?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:17 - 02:34\nNo. No. And to be clear, we don't think that we're gonna be the ones curing the diseases. Our goal is always to build tools that could accelerate the whole scientific field, so that way the scientific field collectively could cure all the diseases. But still people thought that by the end of the century it was a stretch.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:34 - 02:35\nNow I think it's like too conservative.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 02:36 - 02:56\nAnd so we kept being like, okay, well, we had these series of funny, awkward educational conversations where we were like, okay, but like, why? Like, why do you think it's impossible? And like, you know, just being the person in the room is just like, oh, I don't know why. You tell me. Finally, we got people to like, they're like, fine.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 02:56 - 03:08\nIf you really must know. And we're like, you know, we do. It seems important. You know, they were like, well, we work in silos. And when you publish, information doesn't get shared.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 03:08 - 03:32\nIt gets locked up for long periods of time. And we don't have tooling. You know, they gave the example of like, we build a great tool by one postdoc in a lab and it lives on their computer. And when they graduate, the tool is gone. And we heard was very hard to build shared tools to move science faster, build a shared knowledge base to quickly move science faster.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 03:33 - 03:39\nAnd that's sort of where we began in thinking about, okay, like if those are the problems, like what can we contribute?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:40 - 04:03\nYeah. I mean, so the original Biohub model was basically focus on long term tool development by bringing together engineers and scientists across multiple universities to focus on long term tool development. And basically, it like worked. And, you know, we started off with with CZI doing a number of different things. And I think over time, we just felt like, okay.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:03 - 04:42\nThe science piece is really working, and we just kept on investing more and more and more in it until now it is basically the primary and main thing that we're doing. And we'v
  12.1	e expanded the original San Francisco Biohub to a handful now at this point. There's New York. There's Chicago. The real focus and the unifying theme at this point the virtual biology initiative around taking the unique data sets that are able to be generated in order to model effectively starting with the smallest pieces of proteins, but then eventually cells and whole biological systems.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:42 - 05:24\nBut that's kind of how we've evolved, this idea that we talk about around that some of this is an AI problem, and you want to build a frontier AI lab, but you need to couple that with a frontier biology effort that can do the work of basically being able to understand and get the data that you need to actually be able to build these models. Because unlike language models, there's just like a lot of data out there on the Internet. That's not really the case with biology. I mean, there are obviously a bunch of different data sets that exist that academia and scientists have generated over the the decades, but a lot of the stuff that I think we wanna put into this, it doesn't exist. Right?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 05:24 - 06:10\nIt's like you wanna be able to visualize things that people haven't been able to see before, which is why we were doing the the imaging work. You wanna be able to record things that are going on inside the body, which is why we're doing the kind of cellular engineering work. Or you want to be able to measure things like inflammation in ways that haven't been possible, which is why the Chicago Biohub is focused on building those kind of devices and being able to do that. And that will fundamentally create new types of datasets that will allow new types of models. And I think it's just a very exciting thing that, going back to what you're saying, if the scientific field, it primarily needs kind of tool development that now is going to empower scientists across the field to be able to do their work faster, that's what we think we can provide through this kind of long term focus on tool development.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 06:10 - 07:08\nBut I think there's a fun through line on where we started and, you know, bringing us to our work that Alex is driving now, is that our very first request for application, RFA here, was around single cell sequencing. And we wanted to look at sort of like the RNA that is transcribed in individual cells. And that was possible, but it was still pretty early on in understanding how different cells were expressing their DNA to the point where at the beginning we were just funding methods, like getting people to describe how to do it so that others could share that methodology. And then that became us funding the Human Cell Atlas, which is now one of the largest databases of single cell transcriptomes, it was getting hard for scientists to annotate the data.    406	          "text": "Good post on the dynamics of AI pricing right now. We’re basically going to see a barbell dynamic on pricing between high cost frontier models and cheap but good open or closed weights models. \n\nThen the question is how do you maximize efficiency from the frontier models, and get better quality and performance of the cheaper models. That’s where the applied AI layer comes in.\n\nThe applied layer can route to the best model at any time given the workload at hand, as well as mitigate either high token costs or worse performance. The closer you are to the underlying workflow, the better you can tune the use of models for a given business process. \n\nThat’s all about understanding the workflows, domain specific FDEs, evals by customer or process, getting data setup well for agents, and more. Overall good for applied AI.",

# AI Builders Digest — June 24, 2026

## X / TWITTER

**Andrej Karpathy** declared that Claude Tag represents the "3rd major redesign of LLM UI/UX." The first paradigm was the LLM as a website you visit; the second was the LLM as an app you download; the third is "a self-contained, persistent, asynchronous entity with org-wide tools and context, working alongside teams of humans." He described how once you do the engineering work to make it "just work" across tools, integrations, compute environments, memory, and security, "Claude basically joins the team in a seamless way — you can talk to it as you would talk to a person." The post received 12,492 likes — by far the most-engaged tweet in this batch.

> Andrej Karpathy 宣布 Claude Tag 代表了"LLM UI/UX 的第三次重大重新设计。"第一个范式是 LLM 作为你要访问的网站；第二个范式是 LLM 作为你要下载的应用；第三个范式是"一个自包含、持久化、异步的实体，拥有组织级工具和上下文，与人类团队并肩工作。"他描述了当你完成让它在工具、集成、计算环境、内存和安全性之间"正常工作"的工程工作后，"Claude 基本上以无缝的方式加入了团队——你可以像与人交谈一样与它对话。"这条推文获得了 12,492 个赞——是本批中互动量最高的推文。

https://x.com/karpathy/status/2069547676849557725

---

**Anthropic's Boris Cherny** announced Claude Tag for Slack — tag @Claude in a channel, and it spins up an instance with its own sandbox, clones repos, writes code, tests, and compiles in an isolated environment. Each thread gets its own instance with its own memory and permissions. He also demonstrated having Claude proactively monitor Slack channels to answer questions, draft PRs, and react with emojis (✅❌) when threads are resolved. It's in beta for Claude Enterprise and Team customers today.

> Anthropic 的 Boris Cherny 宣布了 Claude Tag for Slack——在频道中 @Claude，它会启动一个带有自己沙箱的实例，克隆仓库、编写代码、测试和编译，全部在隔离环境中完成。每个线程都有自己的实例、内存和权限。他还演示了让 Claude 主动监控 Slack 频道来回答问题、起草 PR，并在线程解决时用表情符号（✅❌）做出反应。该功能今天面向 Claude Enterprise 和 Team 客户开放测试。

https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474691010707486
https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474689819480394
https://x.com/bcherny/status/2069474688619958517

---

**Anthropic's Cat Wu** shared that Claude Tag's internal version at Anthropic merges 65% of product PRs, calling it "a paradigm shift in how we ship products." She emphasized it's "our first product that is natively multi-player and proactive," and shared a guide for configuring agent permissions.

> Anthropic 的 Cat Wu 分享说，Claude Tag 在 Anthropic 内部的版本合并了 65% 的产品 PR，称其为"我们交付产品方式的范式转变。"她强调这是"我们第一个原生支持多人协作和主动性的产品，"并分享了配置 Agent 权限的指南。

https://x.com/_catwu/status/2069473118742331608
https://x.com/_catwu/status/2069484330938998993

---

**Anthropic's Alex Albert** echoed Karpathy's sentiment: "This has completely changed how I work with Claude. It feels less like using a tool and more like managing a team."

> Anthropic 的 Alex Albert 呼应了 Karpathy 的感受："这完全改变了我与 Claude 的合作方式。感觉不像在使用工具，而更像在管理一个团队。"

https://x.com/alexalbert__/status/2069470389391241314

**Anthropic's Thariq** shared practical tips for using Claude Tag — setting up a "scheduling" channel where Claude finds time on calendars, using pinned messages for status tracking, and having Claude react with emojis (⏲️✅❓🛑) so you can see its status at a glance.

> Anthropic 的 Thariq 分享了使用 Claude Tag 的实用技巧——设置一个"日程安排"频道让 Claude 在日历上找时间，使用置顶消息进行状态跟踪，以及让 Claude 用表情符号（⏲️✅❓🛑）做出反应，让你一目了然地看到它的状态。

**OpenAI's Thibault Sottiaux** shared that Codex "had a bug. Fixed." — receiving 3,249 likes and 279 replies, showing the community's appetite for rapid iteration. He also joked about the intensity of working at OpenAI: "If you are at OpenAI, you would know that this never happens. Be right back celebrating 5 minutes of peace."

> OpenAI 的 Thibault Sottiaux 分享说 Codex "之前有个 bug。已修复。"——获得了 3,249 个赞和 279 条回复，显示了社区对快速迭代的渴望。他还调侃了在 OpenAI 工作的强度："如果你在 OpenAI，你会知道这种事永远不会发生。我先去庆祝这 5 分钟的平静。"

**Peter Yang** asked a provocative question: "What is design if what's accessing your product is just an agent looking for an API or CLI?" He also discovered the Google Workspace CLI and called it "super useful," wondering why it isn't celebrated more. And he observed that "human-agent interaction is basically trending towards managing a highly capable employee" — "1 on 1s and performance reviews for Claude next?"

> Peter Yang 提出了一个 provocative 的问题："如果访问你产品的只是一个寻找 API 或 CLI 的 Agent，那设计是什么？"他还发现了 Google Workspace CLI，称其"超级有用"，想知道为什么它没有得到更多关注。他还观察到"人机交互基本上正在朝着管理一名高能力员工的方向发展"——"接下来要给 Claude 做一对一和绩效评估了吗？"

---

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** analyzed the barbell dynamic in AI pricing: high-cost frontier models on one end, cheap open/closed weights models on the other. The applied layer's job is to route between them — maximizing efficiency from frontier models while improving quality from cheaper ones. "The closer you are to the underlying workflow, the better you can tune the use of models for a given business process." He also highlighted Claude Tag's integration with Box — making enterprise content a "portable knowledge base" accessible from Slack.

> Box CEO Aaron Levie 分析了 AI 定价中的杠铃动态：一端是高成本前沿模型，另一端是便宜的开源/闭源权重模型。应用层的工作是在两者之间路由——最大化前沿模型的效率，同时提高廉价模型的质量。"你越接近底层工作流，就越能针对特定业务流程优化模型的使用。"他还强调了 Claude Tag 与 Box 的集成——使企业内容成为可从 Slack 访问的"便携知识库"。

https://x.com/levie/status/2069639600310767616
https://x.com/levie/status/2069596515560267891

He also called out Dropbox for not supporting larger than 3TB plans — "the amount of data we are throwing off that is actually usable is going to go exponential from AI."

> YC CEO Garry Tan 称 Linzumi 是"Codex 但真正支持多人协作——对团队来说很神奇，"并指出其创始人 Sean Grove 曾在 OpenAI 团队大幅减少了 ChatGPT 中的讨好行为。他还指出 Dropbox 不支持超过 3TB 的方案——"我们产生的实际可用的数据量将因 AI 而呈指数级增长。"

## PODCASTS

**No Priors: Biohub — The Future of Biology is Open-Source with Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Alex Rives**

Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Biohub's Head of Science Alex Rives joined No Priors for a deep conversation on building open-source AI for biology at massive scale.

**The origin story**: Chan and Zuckerberg started Biohub ten years ago with the goal to "cure, prevent, and manage all disease by the end of the century." Nobel Prize-winning scientists laughed at them. "Now I think it's too conservative," Zuckerberg said. The early focus was on tool-building — single-cell sequencing, the Human Cell Atlas, and CellByGene — which grew into a corpus used by the entire scientific community.

**The strategy: frontier AI + frontier biology**: Zuckerberg described their approach as building hierarchical world models of biology — starting with proteins, then cells, then whole biological systems. Unlike language models where data is abundant on the internet, biology data must be invented: "You need to invent new novel scientific approaches to be able to observe the things that create the data that go into the model." This is why Biohub combines an AI research team with wet labs doing cellular engineering (New York), imaging (San Francisco), and inflammation measurement devices (Chicago).

**ESM Fold: a world model for proteins**: Alex Rives's team released ESM Fold — a protein language model trained on billions of sequences that learns emergent representations of protein biology. It folded over 1.1 billion proteins and predicted their structures at atomic resolution. The model is blazing fast and hits state-of-the-art on structure prediction, protein-protein interactions, and antibody design. Most striking: they didn't design a model for antibodies — they "just designed a model that could understand proteins, and you get protein design as an emergent property." They validated their designs in the lab with cryo-EM, finding nanomolar binders.

**Mechanistic interpretability in biology**: Rives is applying techniques from LLM interpretability to protein models — opening the black box to understand what biology the model has learned. Because the model trains on billions of sequences spanning known and unknown biology, it develops representations that connect proteins we know nothing about to proteins we understand, revealing "underlying structure grammar."

**Why open source**: Zuckerberg was clear: "We just want to give tools to the whole scientific community." Running it as a nonprofit avoids the constraints of venture funding — the 10-15 year time horizon, the need to invent new science to generate data, and the desire to put tools in the hands of everyone, including researchers working on rare diseases. "Our vision is not that there's going to be some central superintelligence that solves all of science."

**The ultimate goal**: Priscilla Chan framed it as treating the individual as an individual — understanding the full chain from gene variant to protein to disease process, then designing a bespoke intervention. "If we could design a protein to actually change the physiology, then we can actually cure someone." The next big challenge: the virtual cell — modeling each level of biological complexity (proteomic, genetic, transcriptomic) and connecting it to phenotype.

> **No Priors：Biohub——生物学的未来是开源的，与 Mark Zuckerberg、Priscilla Chan 和 Alex Rives 的对话**
>
> Mark Zuckerberg、Priscilla Chan 和 Biohub 科学负责人 Alex Rives 在 No Priors 上进行了一场深度对话，探讨如何大规模构建用于生物学的开源 AI。
>
> **起源故事**：Chan 和 Zuckerberg 十年前创立了 Biohub，目标是"在本世纪末治愈、预防和管理所有疾病。"诺贝尔奖得主科学家们嘲笑他们。Zuckerberg 说："现在我认为这个目标太保守了。"早期的重点是工具建设——单细胞测序、人类细胞图谱和 CellByGene——这些工具后来发展成为整个科学界使用的知识库。
>
> **策略：前沿 AI + 前沿生物学**：Zuckerberg 将他们的方法描述为构建生物学的分层世界模型——从蛋白质开始，然后是细胞，再到整个生物系统。与互联网上数据丰富的语言模型不同，生物学数据必须被创造："你需要发明新的、新颖的科学方法，才能观察到那些构成模型数据的东西。"这就是为什么 Biohub 将 AI 研究团队与进行细胞工程（纽约）、成像（旧金山）和炎症测量设备（芝加哥）的湿实验室结合起来。
>
> **ESM Fold：蛋白质的世界模型**：Alex Rives 的团队发布了 ESM Fold——一个在数十亿条序列上训练的蛋白质语言模型，能够学习蛋白质生物学的涌现表示。它折叠了超过 11 亿个蛋白质，并以原子分辨率预测了它们的结构。该模型速度极快，在结构预测、蛋白质-蛋白质相互作用和抗体设计方面达到了最先进水平。最引人注目的是：他们没有专门设计抗体模型——他们"只是设计了一个能够理解蛋白质的模型，而蛋白质设计作为涌现属性自然产生。"他们在实验室用冷冻电镜验证了设计，发现了纳摩尔级别的结合物。
>
> **生物学中的机制可解释性**：Rives 正在将 LLM 可解释性的技术应用于蛋白质模型——打开黑箱，理解模型学到了什么生物学知识。由于模型在跨越已知和未知生物学的数十亿条序列上训练，它发展出了将我们一无所知的蛋白质与已知蛋白质联系起来的表示，揭示了"底层的结构语法"。
>
> **为什么开源**：Zuckerberg 非常明确："我们只是想给整个科学界提供工具。"以非营利方式运营避免了风险投资的约束——10-15 年的时间跨度、需要发明新科学来生成数据，以及希望将工具交到每个人手中，包括研究罕见疾病的研究人员。"我们的愿景不是会有某个中央超级智能来解决所有科学问题。"
>
> **最终目标**：Priscilla Chan 将其表述为将每个个体作为个体来对待——理解从基因变异到蛋白质再到疾病过程的完整链条，然后设计定制化的干预方案。"如果我们能够设计一种蛋白质来实际改变生理机能，那么我们就能真正治愈一个人。"下一个重大挑战：虚拟细胞——对生物复杂性的每个层面（蛋白质组、基因组、转录组）进行建模，并将其与表型联系起来。

https://www.youtube.com/@NoPriorsPodcast

     2	  "generatedAt": "2026-06-26T07:36:54.643Z",

     5	    {
     6	      "source": "podcast",
     7	      "name": "The MAD Podcast with Matt Turck",
     8	      "title": "Cloudflare CEO: Bot Takeover, Edge AI & The Hard Decision Every CEO Will Face",
     9	      "guid": "5ae96ad9-5cee-4cda-ad01-8edea9dfbc0a",
    10	      "url": "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UN47z_opfmo",
    11	      "publishedAt": "2026-06-25T11:30:00.000Z",
    12	      "transcript": "Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:31\nWe actually had bot traffic pass human traffic online in the first half of twenty twenty six. In five years you might have a thousand times as much traffic on the internet as you do today. In the next six to twelve months, almost every company is going to go through some exercise like this where they're gonna cut a bunch of their their team. The underlying business model of the internet for the last twenty eight years has remained basically the same, which is it's largely advertising. The problem is bots don't click on ads.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 00:31 - 00:34\nOver the next five years, the business model of the Internet is gonna change radically.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 00:34 - 00:50\nHi. I'm Matt Turk. Welcome to the Mad Podcast. Today, I'm excited to welcome Matthew Prince, cofounder and CEO of Cloudflare, one of the Internet's most important companies that sits in front of a very large portion of global Internet traffic. We start the conversation by unpacking a huge milestone.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 00:50 - 01:25\nFor the first time, the number of bots and AI agents has outnumbered humans on the Internet. We also cover how Cloudflare has become an AI infrastructure company and dig into Cloudflare workers, AI gateway, and agent security. We also don't shy away from some of the more controversial topics, including Cloudflare's recent decision to rightsize its team to adapt to the new AI era. I should add that Matthew is a wonderful storyteller, and he shares some truly wild and hilarious episodes from Cloudflare's journey from scrappy startup to the massive public company it is today. Please enjoy this fantastic conversation with Matthew.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 01:27 - 01:48\nI want to start with something that feels like a very crucial important moment in the history of the Internet, what you tweeted a couple of weeks ago and you said effectively that the number of bots and machines on the Internet now outnumbers humans. So can you unpack that and explain what that was?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 01:48 - 02:33\nYeah. So I mean there have always been bots that ran around online because the way Google works is they scrape the Internet and they then build their index based on that. And historically throughout most of the history of Cloudflare, it's about 20% of traffic on the Internet was bots and that held fairly consistent over a period of time until until about two years ago when we started to see a real rise in bots driven mainly by AI and and the LLMs and everything that was going on behind those. And last fall, in the fall of twenty twenty five, I was asked at what point will automated traffic pass human traffic? And we pulled the data and we sort of extrapolated out the trends and we thought that it was going to be the end of twenty twenty seven.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 02:33 - 03:13\nFast forward to South by Southwest this year, March 2026 and I was asked the same question again and we looked at the data again and we're like, oh wow, it's growing a lot faster than we expected. It's moved now to the first half of twenty twenty seven. And then, you know, I was pretty shocked when my team came to me just a few weeks ago and they said, won't believe it, but we actually had bot traffic pass human traffic online in the first half of twenty twenty six. So I think what that shows is just how fast these different systems, again almost all driven by AI, are growing and they're really driving so much more traffic across the internet.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 03:13 - 03:17\nAnd what was the denominator? Was it like a HTTP request?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:17 - 03:51\nYeah. So so Cloudflare sits in front of, you know, a huge percentage of the Internet and so we and it's in a super representative sample. And and so as a result, we can always look at what are the general trends across the Internet and we have a site we call radar. If you go to radar.cloudflow.com, this tracking a number of different things and one of those is how much human traffic is there, how much bot traffic is there and in in the last couple months it it the the bot traffic exceeded the human traffic. And I and I I think that if you extrapolate this out, you know, in five years, I think you'll see a thousand times more bot traffic than human traffic.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 03:51 - 04:05\nAnd if I had to take kind of an over under bet on that, I'd take the over bet that it's just gonna it's it's growing at such an exponential rate that that you're just gonna see an enormous amount of the Internet that's driven by by by these by these various bots and agents and other systems.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 04:05 - 04:10\nAnd when we say bots, do we mean agents? Do we mean crawling bots? What\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:10 - 04:38\ndo I we mean I think that agent, bot, crawler are all synonyms. It all means the same thing. It depends on whether you want to describe it in a sort of normatively positive way in which case you call it an agent. If you want to describe it kind of the ickiest way you call 
  12.1	it a crawler or a scraper, but it's the same thing behind the scenes. It's it's a it's a machine that's accessing resources as opposed to kind of a human eyeball driving a browser accessing those those resources.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 04:38 - 05:28\nAnd and again in this particular case, you still have a whole bunch of this sort of scraper crawler hacker traffic that hasn't changed that much. You still have some of the sort of bots like Google bot that are that are building search indexes that hasn't changed that much. If anything has gone a little bit down. But the thing that's really driving this is what I think we would all call agents, which is you know if if I go to ChatGPT or I go to Claude or I go to you know, any of these systems and and I'm shopping for a digital camera, you're see, you know, thousands of times more site visits for that same activity, that same job to be done of of shopping for a digital camera than if I did it myself. I I might visit five sites if I'm, you know, personally trying to figure out what digital camera to buy, whereas my agent might visit 5,000 and that's what's really driving this increased demand.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 05:28 - 05:43\nYeah, and what does that change from, I guess, an infrastructure perspective? Like what you just described, 5,000 sites versus five. In some ways, that sounds inefficient. I mean, it's efficient, for me as a user ultimately because I guess the best answer. What does that change?\n\nSpeaker 2 | 05:43 - 05:45\nIn terms of requirements for the infrastructure?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 05:45 - 06:11\nYeah. Think, I mean, first of all, there there if if this trend continues, there's just gonna be an enormous amount of additional demand on on the Internet. And I go back to, you know, during COVID where over the course of two weeks we saw Internet traffic double. You know, if if if our projections are right, that's gonna seem quaint very soon. You know, in five years, you might have a thousand times as much traffic on the internet as you do today.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 06:11 - 06:52\nAnd that's gonna mean more, you know, servers, more network infrastructure, CPUs, more GPUs, more memory, all of the things that have to serve these bots are are gonna be very important. And it's also gonna mean we have to figure out some new business model in order to fund that. Someone has to pay for it and and you know the the business model of the Internet historically has been ads and bots don't click on ads. So it's it's going to be something different going forward and you know, I I think the most interesting question that in in the world today is over the next five years, the business model of the Internet is gonna change radically and what it changes to is totally undefined at this point.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 06:52 - 07:00\nYeah. This whole new vocabulary It used to be clicks and views and what is it going to be now? Like actions or?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 07:00 - 07:31\nWe've got to figure out exactly what that is and it's going to, you know, change a number of things. Like, you know, what is a brand in an agentic world? You know, a brand historically has been, you know way as a as a shortcut to tell humans kind of this is something that has a certain set of expectations will have a certain set of experience associated with it. If I if I see a Walmart logo on the side of a building, I know what it's gonna be what like when I walk in. If I see a Marriott logo, I see a McDonald's logo, I know what that's going to be like.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 07:31 - 08:11\nYou know, the the thing about the thing about bots, they have infinite amount of patients in order to discover you know everything that that might be might be right. So the basic idea of what a brand is going forward is going to be very different than what it has been in what had been the very much human driven kind of commerce and Internet experience. And and again, I'm not sure I'm not sure that I have I have ideas of what that might look like and and I think that Kleffler will play some role in in helping figure that out. But I think it's all going get discovered over the next five years and that's incredibly exciting.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 08:11 - 08:20\nAlright, fascinating. I'd love to spend a little bit of time on Cloudflare 01/2001. If I put Cloudflare in front of my website, what happens?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 08:21 - 09:38\nYeah, so, know, what Cloudflare started out as was how can you take a firewall, like a security product and put it in the cloud. The original idea came from, you know a realization that you know software was moving the cloud, servers were moving in the cloud, therefore all the security and networking infrastructure had to move to the cloud as well. And so that was the basic premise that that we started with and so if you signed up for Cloudflare fifteen years ago and you put us in front of your website, what you were effectively doing is is essentially putting a bouncer in front of the website that could check anyone who was trying to come 
  12.2	to it and then stop the bad ones and then because we were we we needed to make sure we didn't slow things down, we would also then, take a lot of load off of your website, and and be able to, you know, for for good visitors deliver the content significantly faster. So the original idea of Cloudflare was sort of performance and security in a very easy to adopt package. We knew as a business that in order to succeed we eventually had to sell our services to you know major banks and governments and health all the things that we are now count as customers.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 09:38 - 10:29\nBut in the early days, those things didn't they were never going to trust us because we were a dopey little startup and had, you know, we had no infrastructure, we had no data to actually provide the security services. And so the thing that we did was we created a free sort of stripped down version of the service with the idea of making it available to everyone. What was interesting, we expected it was going to be a bunch of like, you know, SMBs and startups and and and individuals that would sign up. But it turns out that, you know, if you think of it sort of x y axis and you know, the x axis is is budget and the y axis is is like security issues. There's almost a perfectly diagonal line that the smaller your budget is the fewer security issues you have, the bigger your budget is the more security issues you have.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 10:29 - 11:09\nAnd so it turned out that the we even SMB startups wouldn't sign up even though it was free. But two organizations kind of defy that or two two groups defy that. One was was Hacker Kids because they're all always attacking each other and so overnight every hacker kid in the world signed up for for Cloudflare and and and then they were attacking each other trying to take us down. And then the other were you know, sort of human rights organizations, humanitarian organizations, nonprofits. Because if you're doing any of that work, turns out nine times out of 10 you're pissing somebody off and oftentimes it's you know a government or something that will then attack you.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 11:09 - 11:32\nSo overnight we had like every humanitarian organization in the world, every hacker kid in the world using Cloudflare. They were all, know, and we all of a sudden came under just massive attacks. And so we were constantly trying to figure out how do we stay in front of it? How do we actually continue to to exist and thrive in this world? And that that forced us to build, you know, a number of different things.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 11:32 - 12:25\nSo we, you know, built a registrar product so we could register domains because we had used a registrar and we came this close to our domain getting stolen, which would have been a disaster for us. We had to build DDoS mitigation, which initially we didn't think we wanted to build at all. We had to build, you know, a DNS infrastructure because we couldn't get anyone to support us. Needed to build our own sort of VPN because everybody else was too slow or not secure enough. And eventually we had to build our own developer platform where you could where our team could turn around and actually take the ideas that they had and deploy them in a way that was that was able to be you know incredibly limited sandboxed and other things because you know at some point we got big enough that if we had an issue it was it was incredibly you know, disruptive to the world in terms of what we're doing.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 12:25 - 13:07\nAnd so in the end the real story of Cloudflare has been, know, we started with this idea that was pretty simple which was how do you put a firewall in the cloud and then that and then we sort of created so many problems over time for ourselves that we had to solve. And then in the process of solving those problems, we've built a whole series of products. And so Cloudflare today unlike when we started, you know, I mean I have a hard time articulating exactly what it is that we do. At some level we, know, we're sort of the next generation sort of AWS or Google Cloud where we've rethought how that works. We still provide security and performance across that.\n\nSpeaker 1 | 13:07 - 13:37\nBut we have additional products that take advantage of the network that we have in order to provide things like a cloud based VPN and other things. And and you know, we're increasingly because of the massive percentage of the Internet that we sit in front of, we're increasingly sort of playing a role in figuring out what's that business model of of the Internet of the future. And so our our mission is to help build a better Internet and I think anything that sort of is in fulfillment of that mission is is the sort of problem that we're always thinking about.\n\nSpeaker 2 | 13:37 - 13:53\nYeah, the concept of network is fascinating and seems very central to the story. And it's a network of users, but it's also physical network. Right? Like, what what you guys did initially 
  12.3	was to create this edge network. Like, how does one even go about building that as a young startup?\n\nSpeaker 1 | 13:53 - 15:00\nI mean, in the very beginning, you know, we when we when we launched, we had servers in five cities around the world. The Chicago, Ashburn, Virginia, San Jose, California, Amsterdam, and Tokyo.
